proposals.title: DART: Dynamic Articulated Reconstruction and Tracking from Monocular Video
proposals.motivation: Existing methods for reconstructing articulated objects, like CARTO, operate on static scenes. For robots to interact with everyday objects like tools, appliances, or cabinet doors in dynamic, real-world settings, they must perceive and model not just static shape but also the motion and articulation of objects from video. This capability is critical for manipulation planning and long-horizon tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by extending category-agnostic articulated reconstruction with a temporal tracking module and a learned dynamics prior, we can robustly estimate the full 3D pose, shape, and articulation parameters of novel objects from monocular video, even under egomotion and partial occlusion.
proposals.method: We will build upon the CARTO framework, which recovers shape and articulation from single images. We will integrate a per-frame encoder with a temporal transformer to establish correspondences and smooth estimates across time. A key innovation will be a factored latent representation separating static shape, time-varying articulation state, and global motion. We will train the system on synthetic video sequences of articulated objects in motion, using a combination of 3D supervision and self-supervised losses on optical flow and temporal consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first method for category-agnostic, joint-agnostic 3D reconstruction and tracking of articulated objects from monocular video. It will provide a foundational perception module for dynamic robotic manipulation. We will release a new benchmark dataset of real and synthetic videos with ground-truth articulation trajectories for evaluation.
proposals.novelty: While CARTO handles static articulation, and other works track rigid objects, jointly solving for unknown articulation structure and its dynamic state over time from a single moving camera is a novel and significantly more challenging problem. The integration of a learned dynamics model within a reconstruction pipeline is a key non-obvious advancement.

proposals.title: CLOCC: Continuous Latent Occupancy Fields for Task-Driven Parsing of Extreme Visual Clutter
proposals.motivation: Robust robot manipulation in unstructured, cluttered environments like piles, bins, or tangles remains a core challenge. Pre-2026 perception systems, often focused on instance-level detection, fail when objects are heavily occluded, deformed, or lack distinct visual features. A paradigm shift is needed from recognizing discrete objects to modeling the continuous physical structure of all matter in a scene to support functional interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a continuous 4D (3D + time) amodal occupancy field, trained with multi-modal sensing and physics-informed synthetic data, can model the complete latent structure and dynamics of cluttered scenes, enabling a downstream task-specific reasoner to segment actionable volumes from natural language or functional prompts.
proposals.method: We will extend recent neural implicit representations (e.g., NeRF, Occupancy Networks) to model a time-conditioned, amodal occupancy field from RGB-D video and proprioceptive data (inspired by HANDLOOM's tracing of 1D structures). Training will leverage large-scale synthetic datasets of piled and tangled objects, generated with physics simulators, to learn occlusion reasoning. A separate, modular reasoning network, conditioned on language embeddings (e.g., from CLIP) and trained with AI feedback methods (as critiqued in Kollar's prior work), will query this field to output segmented 3D regions for tasks like 'clear' or 'retrieve', similar to CARTO's agnostic reconstruction but for functional segmentation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel scene representation for extreme clutter that models latent occupancy and motion, 2) a framework for task-driven, volumetric parsing of undifferentiated matter, and 3) a benchmark for evaluating manipulation-centric perception in highly occluded settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in combining continuous amodal scene fields with a separate, language-conditioned task reasoner for clutter parsing, moving beyond instance detection to generate executable volumetric structures based on physics and intent, which is distinct from prior category-agnostic reconstruction or single-object tracking.

proposals.title: SCoPE: Scene Compositional Physics Engine from Single-View Observation
proposals.motivation: Current scene understanding methods are fragmented. Holistic scene reconstruction (e.g., from NeRF or SLAM) yields static geometry but lacks actionable object semantics, while detailed object models (e.g., articulated reconstruction like CARTO) ignore broader scene context and physical constraints. For robots to perform complex tasks in human environments, they need a unified representation that bridges fine-grained functional part understanding with room-scale physical reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: A unified model that jointly infers the compositional hierarchy of articulated object parts and the room-scale scene layout will enable more accurate and physically-consistent predictions of interaction affordances and dynamics than systems treating these problems separately.
proposals.method: We propose a two-level neural architecture. First, a bottom-up stream, extending HANDLOOM and CARTO, extracts 1D/articulated structures and functional parts from RGB-D video. Second, a top-down stream, using a 3D graph neural network operating on a scene graph, integrates these parts into object and room-level representations, enforcing physical consistency (e.g., attachment, collision, support). We will train the system using a combination of simulated data (e.g., SAPIEN) and self-supervised real-world interaction data, where robot actions (like flinging or pulling cables) provide signals to refine kinematic and dynamic property estimation.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel scene representation unifying fine-grained articulation with holistic 3D layout. 2) A method for jointly inferring this representation from single-view observation. 3) Demonstrated improvements on downstream tasks like affordance prediction, manipulation planning, and scene rearrangement simulation compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight, bi-directional coupling between part-level kinematic inference and global scene physics, moving beyond treating articulated object reconstruction and scene reconstruction as independent problems.

proposals.title: FUSE: Functional Understanding for Synthesis and Editing via Disentangled Priors from Web-Scale 3D Data
proposals.motivation: Current 3D generative models, trained on large datasets like Objaverse, primarily capture geometric and textural patterns but lack a robust understanding of object semantics and function. This limits their utility for robotics and design, where an object's purpose (e.g., 'pour-able', 'sit-able') is paramount. A model that intrinsically links form to function would enable more intuitive, semantically-aware 3D content creation and manipulation.
proposals.hypothesis: A generative model trained on web-scale 3D data with explicit disentanglement objectives can learn a latent space where geometry, texture, and functional semantics are separable, enabling high-level semantic and affordance-based control over 3D synthesis and editing.
proposals.method: We will train a diffusion-based 3D generator (e.g., a 3D-aware variant of Diffusion Transformer) on Objaverse and ShapeNet. Building on disentanglement techniques from CARTO, we will structure the latent space using contrastive and adversarial losses to separate geometry, appearance, and semantic/functional vectors derived from textual descriptions and automated affordance labeling. The model will be conditioned on CLIP embeddings, with a novel 'functional guidance' mechanism that steers generation based on affordance labels (e.g., 'stable', 'graspable') extracted via language models from web data.
proposals.contribution: 1) A foundational 3D generative model with a semantically and functionally disentangled prior. 2) A benchmark and protocol for evaluating functional awareness in 3D synthesis. 3) Demonstrated applications in intuitive 3D editing for design and generating functionally-grounded objects for robotic simulation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work disentangles shape and appearance, we explicitly disentangle and model functional semantics as a core latent factor, leveraging web-scale data to learn the non-linear mapping from form to purpose, which is novel for holistic 3D generation.

proposals.title: CABLE-GRASP: Category-Agnostic Bimanual Learning for Efficient Single-Example Grasping of Novel Deformable Objects
proposals.motivation: Robotic manipulation of novel, deformable objects like cables, ropes, or cloth remains a significant challenge, as their high-dimensional state spaces and complex dynamics defy traditional rigid-body planning. Pre-2026 methods often require extensive per-object training data or precise models, which are impractical for real-world deployment where a robot encounters a new object only once. This work addresses the critical gap in enabling robots to learn effective manipulation strategies from minimal interaction with a single novel instance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the category-agnostic reconstruction and state estimation principles from CARTO and HANDLOOM can be extended and integrated with a learned manipulation policy to enable robust, single-example grasping of novel deformable objects, by using the first interaction to build an instance-specific model that informs subsequent grasp planning.
proposals.method: We will adapt the joint-agnostic, single-view reconstruction network from CARTO to rapidly build a coarse 3D deformable model of a novel object from a single RGB-D observation. This model will be combined with the 1D tracing and state estimation from HANDLOOM to parameterize the object's state. A bimanual manipulation policy, inspired by dynamic actions like 'fling', will be trained via reinforcement learning in simulation, using the reconstructed model and estimated state as input. The policy will be fine-tuned on a real robot using a single real-world interaction, guided by a learned prior from simulation and AI feedback for sample-efficient alignment of behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a unified framework for single-example perception and manipulation of novel deformable objects, 2) empirical validation on real-robot tasks like untangling and grasping cables, and 3) a public dataset of deformable object interactions to benchmark category-agnostic manipulation. It advances the field towards robots that can adapt to new objects without extensive re-training.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight, closed-loop integration of a one-shot category-agnostic reconstruction module with a dynamic manipulation policy, enabling adaptation from a single real-world example—a significant departure from prior work that typically separates perception and action or requires category-specific training.

proposals.title: ROBIN: Robot-Only Bootstrapping of Interaction-Centric Neural Representations
proposals.motivation: Current robot learning often leverages large-scale human video datasets (e.g., Ego4D, Something-Something) to pre-train visual representations for manipulation. However, these videos exhibit a fundamental domain gap: human hands and object interactions differ kinematically and dynamically from robot grippers. This mismatch can limit the efficiency and final performance of policies learned from such representations. A method to learn visual representations directly from in-domain robot interaction data is needed to close this reality gap.
proposals.hypothesis: Visual representations for robotic manipulation can be learned effectively from robot-only interaction data, without human video, by leveraging temporal consistency and physical interaction signals as a self-supervisory signal. Such representations will outperform those pre-trained on human video for downstream robot control tasks.
proposals.method: We will collect a large dataset of robot interaction sequences using a method inspired by 'fling' for cloth and 'cables' for deformables, extended to rigid object pushing and grasping. We will employ a self-supervised learning framework based on contrastive learning (e.g., Time-Contrastive Networks, CURL) or a reconstruction objective (e.g., using a transformer-based video autoencoder). The key is to use temporal consistency of object 'state' and the robot's proprioceptive 'time' series as the learning signal, enforcing that representations are consistent across small time steps where physical properties are preserved. The architecture will build on the category-agnostic reconstruction principles of CARTO and the 1D tracing of HANDLOOM to handle novel objects.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) A novel, large-scale robot interaction dataset for self-supervised representation learning. 2) The ROBIN framework, a method to learn visual representations purely from robot data. 3) Empirical evidence that robot-only representations can match or exceed the performance of human-video-pretrained models on a suite of manipulation benchmarks, redefining the data prerequisites for robot learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses human video or sim-to-real, we propose bootstrapping visual representations solely from real robot interaction streams, treating the robot's own experience as the primary learning resource. This directly tests the necessity of human video, a largely unchallenged assumption in pre-2026 visuomotor learning.

proposals.title: Learning to Manipulate Novel Objects from Noisy Language Supervision via Self-Consistent Visual Grounding
proposals.motivation: Scaling robot learning to open-world environments requires leveraging large, diverse datasets, but high-quality, manually annotated language descriptions for physical interactions are a major bottleneck. Pre-2026, methods often rely on clean, curated captions, which are expensive and scarce, or operate in a purely self-supervised manner, lacking semantic grounding. This work addresses the critical need for robots to learn effective manipulation policies from the abundant but noisy language data (e.g., automated captions, minimal tags, or web text) already associated with many video and interaction datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a robot can learn to manipulate novel objects by integrating noisy, automated language annotations with multi-view visual observations and physical interaction data, using a self-consistency objective to distill a robust, actionable visual-language representation for policy learning.
proposals.method: We will extend the joint reconstruction and representation learning principles from CARTO to the language domain. Our model will consist of a multi-modal encoder (vision, proprioception, and noisy text) and a policy decoder. We will employ a two-stage training process: first, a self-supervised pre-training phase using time-contrastive and state-prediction objectives on interaction videos (inspired by fling and state dynamics modeling), and second, a denoising phase where a lightweight caption refinement module is trained via cycle-consistency between predicted actions from text and observed interaction outcomes. Specific architectures will build upon pre-2026 vision-language models (e.g., CLIP variants) and transformer-based policy networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel framework for learning manipulation policies from minimally curated language-video datasets, 2) a benchmark for evaluating robotic learning under noisy language supervision, and 3) empirical evidence on the sufficiency of automated captions for learning actionable object-centric representations, advancing towards scalable robot learning in the wild.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly tackling the noise in automated language annotations for robotic manipulation, moving beyond the assumption of clean labels or no language at all, by integrating physical interaction consistency as a denoising signal—a direction less explored pre-2026 compared to vision-only or clean-text learning.

proposals.title: Physical Grounding for Semantic Categories: Learning Object Affordances and Dynamics from Unstructured Interaction
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) learn rich semantic categories from text, but these representations lack grounding in physical properties, which is crucial for robotics. While vision provides appearance, physical interaction reveals functional properties like rigidity, deformability, and affordances. Current methods for learning such grounded semantics are data-inefficient and often rely on pre-defined object sets or supervision.
proposals.hypothesis: An embodied agent can learn semantically meaningful object categories and affordances more efficiently by using physical interaction rewards and learned dynamics models as a primary grounding signal, complementing or surpassing visual-linguistic alignment alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the HANDLOOM framework for tracing 1D objects to learn latent representations from robot interaction sequences (e.g., poking, flinging, pulling as in prior work). A contrastive learning objective will align these interaction-derived embeddings with semantic embeddings from a frozen LLM (e.g., CLIP or a language model). We will use the CARTO method's category-agnostic approach to handle novel articulated objects, training a dynamics model to predict interaction outcomes (state, time) which serves as an intrinsic reward for exploring object properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a method for learning physically grounded semantic embeddings from limited interaction data. It will demonstrate that interaction-based rewards can structure a semantic space that aligns with human intuition about object categories and affordances, validated on robotic manipulation tasks with novel objects.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work aligning vision and language, we propose using predicted physical interaction outcomes (learned dynamics) as the core signal for grounding semantics. This integrates the researcher's expertise in learning from interaction (cables, fling) with category-agnostic reconstruction (CARTO) to move beyond passive perception.

proposals.title: VOLTRON-EGO: Language-Driven 3D Scene and Object Representations from Egocentric and Multi-View Video
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, vision-language models like VOLTRON have shown promise for learning generalizable visual representations, but are primarily applied to static, third-person images. For embodied AI and robotics, agents must understand scenes and objects from dynamic, first-person perspectives and across multiple camera views. Scaling VOLTRON's framework to these data modalities is critical for learning representations useful for real-world interactive tasks like manipulation and navigation.
proposals.hypothesis: Extending the VOLTRON framework with a transformer-based multi-view fusion module and temporal contrastive learning will yield 3D-aware visual representations from egocentric and multi-view video that significantly outperform single-view, image-pretrained baselines on downstream robotic perception tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the VOLTRON architecture by replacing its ViT backbone with a video transformer (e.g., TimeSformer) to process egocentric video clips. For multi-view data, we will introduce a cross-view attention module to fuse features from synchronized cameras into a unified scene representation. The model will be trained using a temporal extension of VOLTRON's language-driven reconstruction objective, reconstructing masked spatio-temporal patches and aligning video clips with paired language descriptions from datasets like Ego4D or Habitat's multi-view renders.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a publicly available pre-trained model, VOLTRON-EGO, that provides strong, temporally consistent visual features for embodied AI benchmarks. It will demonstrate a scalable method for integrating language supervision with multi-view and temporal visual data, advancing the field towards more holistic 3D scene understanding from active perception.
proposals.novelty: While VOLTRON operates on single images, this proposal uniquely combines its language-driven paradigm with explicit multi-view fusion and temporal modeling, targeting the specific challenges of egocentric perception—a non-trivial extension given the domain shift and need for 3D reasoning.

proposals.title: VLM-Prompted Manipulation: In-Context Learning for Language-Conditioned Deformable and Articulated Object Handling
proposals.motivation: Robotic manipulation of novel, non-rigid, and articulated objects remains a significant challenge, especially under open-ended language instructions. Pre-2026, while large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) possess rich world knowledge, effectively grounding this knowledge for precise, low-level robot control is non-trivial. This work addresses the gap between high-level VLM semantic understanding and the execution of complex physical manipulation policies for objects like cables (from HANDLOOM) and articulated items (from CARTO).
proposals.hypothesis: That in-context learning and structured prompt engineering with pre-trained, frozen VLMs can significantly improve the generalization and success rate of language-conditioned policies for manipulating novel deformable and articulated objects, without requiring extensive task-specific fine-tuning.
proposals.method: We will build on a state-based or image-based policy backbone (e.g., diffusion policies or state-conditioned networks). The core method involves designing a prompting architecture that feeds the VLM (e.g., CLIP, Flamingo, or GPT-4V) with in-context examples of successful manipulation trajectories (state sequences or keyframes paired with language commands) for related objects. For a new language command, the VLM will be prompted to infer or score sub-goals, which will be translated into constraints or rewards for the low-level policy. We will specifically test this on cable tracing/reconfiguration and articulated object exploration tasks, using the real-world data and models from HANDLOOM and CARTO as testbeds.
proposals.contribution: 1) A novel framework for leveraging frozen VLMs as in-context planners for robotic manipulation, reducing the need for large-scale robotic demonstration data per task. 2) Empirical validation on complex, non-rigid object manipulation benchmarks, demonstrating improved generalization to novel objects and language instructions compared to standard end-to-end or behavior-cloned language-conditioned policies.
proposals.novelty: While VLMs have been used for high-level planning, applying in-context learning specifically to guide the low-level state or action sequences for deformable and articulated object manipulation is a novel direction. This bridges the researcher's expertise in novel object manipulation with cutting-edge (pre-2026) VLM prompting techniques, moving beyond using VLMs solely for perception or top-level task specification.

proposals.title: Embodied Instruction Following for Deformable Linear Object Manipulation via Scene Memory Graphs
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 embodied AI has developed robust methods for instruction-following and spatial scene memory in navigation tasks, but these are rarely applied to complex manipulation domains. Simultaneously, robot manipulation of deformable one-dimensional objects (e.g., cables, ropes) remains a significant challenge, requiring reasoning over dynamic state and task history. Bridging these fields can address the critical gap of executing long-horizon, language-guided tasks with deformable objects.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the instruction-following and topological scene memory representations from embodied visual-language (VL) navigation to the domain of deformable linear object (DLO) manipulation, we can enable a robot to reliably execute complex, multi-step natural language commands involving DLOs.
proposals.method: We will extend the HANDLOOM framework for DLO state estimation with a scene memory graph, inspired by pre-2026 VL navigation models like Episodic Transformer. This graph will encode the DLO's topological state (knots, contacts) and semantic context over time. A language-conditioned policy, trained via imitation or reinforcement learning, will use this memory to ground instructions like 'fling the cable to untangle it and then thread it through the loop.' We will leverage simulation benchmarks (e.g., SoftGym) and real-world experiments with cable/rope manipulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework integrating embodied AI memory architectures into deformable object manipulation, 2) a benchmark for language-guided, long-horizon DLO tasks, and 3) empirical validation on complex instructions requiring state history reasoning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the cross-domain transfer of embodied navigation memory models to the dynamics-heavy problem of DLO manipulation, creating a persistent, language-grounded scene representation for a novel object class where it has not been systematically applied.

proposals.title: ROBODISTILL: Efficient Knowledge Transfer from Foundational Vision-Language Models for Novel Object Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Large pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs) like CLIP or Flamingo possess rich, generalizable representations of objects and scenes, but their size and computational cost make them impractical for real-time robotic control. Conversely, efficient robotics models often struggle with novel objects due to limited training data. Bridging this gap is crucial for enabling robots to understand and manipulate a wide variety of objects in unstructured environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distilling the dense, semantic feature representations from a massive, frozen VLM into a smaller, robotics-specific model via task-aligned losses will yield a policy that significantly outperforms training from scratch, especially for manipulation tasks involving novel object categories.
proposals.method: We will use a large, pre-trained VLM (e.g., CLIP-ViT/L) as a static teacher. A smaller student network, based on a ResNet or ViT-Base backbone, will be trained for manipulation policies (e.g., via imitation or reinforcement learning). Knowledge distillation will be applied at multiple levels: (1) a feature alignment loss matching intermediate spatial feature maps between teacher and student, (2) a contrastive loss on object-centric embeddings to preserve semantic relationships, and (3) integration of distilled features into a temporal action prediction head, building on methods like CARTO for state estimation. Tasks will involve novel object manipulation inspired by prior work on cables and flinging.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a publicly available framework for VLM-to-robot distillation and a benchmark for novel object manipulation. It will provide empirical evidence on the transferability of web-scale visual knowledge to embodied control tasks, advancing sample efficiency and generalization in robot learning.
proposals.novelty: While distillation is common in NLP and vision, its application to distill foundational VLMs specifically for low-level, temporal robot manipulation policies is under-explored. The novelty lies in the multi-modal, spatio-temporal distillation strategy tailored for dynamic interaction, moving beyond static image classification.

proposals.title: DUAL-CABLE: Learning Object-Centric Representations from Single-Trial Robot Interaction via Self-Supervised Dual Objectives
proposals.motivation: Learning generalizable representations for novel object manipulation remains a core challenge in robotics. Pre-2026, methods often rely on large, curated datasets or extensive simulation, which do not capture the rich, in-domain physics of real robot interaction. A framework that can learn from sparse, real-world trial-and-error data—where outcomes are often binary (success/failure)—would enable more efficient adaptation to new objects and tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: A dual-objective learning framework, combining a self-supervised reward signal derived from physical interaction outcomes with a state reconstruction objective, can learn robust, object-centric representations from a single robot interaction trial per object, improving downstream manipulation performance.
proposals.method: We will extend the DUAL framework to physical robot data. A robot will execute a parameterized 'fling' or 'cable-traverse' action (inspired by HANDLOOM) on various novel deformable or articulated objects. The framework will use two heads on a shared visual encoder: 1) a reward prediction head trained with a temporal contrastive loss on successful vs. failed interaction sequences, and 2) a state reconstruction head, informed by CARTO, to predict key object states (e.g., pose, configuration). The dual loss will force the encoder to distill physically-grounded features from limited trials.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a method for sample-efficient representation learning directly from in-domain robot trials, reducing reliance on simulation or massive datasets. It will provide empirical results on manipulation tasks with novel objects, demonstrating improved sample efficiency and generalization over single-objective or pre-trained baselines.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the dual-objective framework specifically to the challenging domain of single-trial, real-robot interaction data with self-supervised rewards, bridging the gap between representation learning for articulated objects and trial-and-error physical learning.

proposals.title: Grounded Instruction Following via Online Articulation and Dynamics Discovery
proposals.motivation: Robots operating in homes or warehouses must execute commands like "fling the cable over the bar" or "open the drawer" in novel environments with unknown object properties. Pre-2026 methods, including the researcher's prior work on HANDLOOM and CARTO, often assume a known or static world model, limiting real-world applicability. Enabling robots to ground language in environments where the physical interaction model must be discovered online is crucial for robust autonomy.
proposals.hypothesis: A robot can ground natural language commands in novel environments by interleaving targeted physical interaction to discover articulation and dynamics models with task-driven planning, outperforming methods that assume a complete prior model.
proposals.method: We will extend the category-agnostic articulation reconstruction of CARTO and the 1D object tracing of HANDLOOM into an online, active discovery framework. The robot will parse a command into a symbolic goal and an uncertainty-aware interaction policy. Using a library of exploratory actions (e.g., poking, pulling, flinging), it will perform targeted tests to estimate object state, articulation trees, and dynamics parameters (like cable stiffness). This discovered model will then inform a task planner (e.g., a PDDL solver with learned predicates) to generate the final execution trajectory.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified framework for online world model building and task grounding, demonstrated on manipulation tasks with articulated and deformable objects. It will provide a benchmark and methodology for evaluating how well robots can compensate for missing prior physical knowledge through targeted interaction.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the tight, closed-loop coupling of language grounding with active physical discovery of object properties, moving beyond static reconstruction (CARTO) or known-model planning. It treats the world model not as a prerequisite but as a variable to be efficiently estimated through task-relevant interaction.

proposals.title: Learning to Interpret Novel Command Structures for Robotic Manipulation via Grounded Language Decomposition
proposals.motivation: Robots trained on fixed command structures (e.g., 'pick up the X') fail in real-world human interaction, where instructions are often idiomatic ('clear the table'), novel ('fling the cable over there'), or non-compositional. This semantic gap, evident in pre-2026 embodied AI benchmarks, severely limits the utility and adaptability of learned manipulation policies in unstructured environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a robot can interpret and execute semantically novel commands by decomposing them into a sequence of grounded, executable primitive actions inferred from visual context and a learned model of language pragmatics, even when the full command phrase is unseen.
proposals.method: We will extend a vision-language model (e.g., CLIP or a pre-2026 VLM) with a neuro-symbolic planning module. The system first parses a novel command using a fine-tuned language model to extract potential action verbs and object referents, grounding them in the current scene via the methods from CARTO and HANDLOOM. A probabilistic grammar, learned from a corpus of diverse instructions, will infer the most likely sequence of known primitive actions (e.g., from 'fling' to a dynamic 'swing-and-release' motor skill) to satisfy the inferred intent, leveraging state and time representations from prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a benchmark for evaluating robotic instruction following with novel semantic structures, and 2) a neuro-symbolic framework that disentangles language interpretation from policy execution, improving generalization. It directly advances the goal of robots that understand intent rather than just command syntax.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that maps commands directly to skills, our approach explicitly models the pragmatic inference between unfamiliar language and a library of grounded primitives, combining the strengths of large-scale pre-training with structured, compositional reasoning for manipulation.

proposals.title: Probabilistic Language-to-Action: Grounding Instructions under Noisy Perception and State Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: Robotic language grounding typically assumes perfect object detection and known spatial relations, which fails in unstructured environments where perception is inherently noisy. This limits the deployment of instruction-following robots to controlled settings. A system that can reason about perceptual uncertainty and ambiguous spatial language is crucial for robust real-world operation.
proposals.hypothesis: By explicitly modeling perception and state estimation as probabilistic processes within a language grounding framework, a robot can generate more robust manipulation policies that are tolerant to detection errors and ambiguous spatial references.
proposals.method: We will extend a language-conditioned policy or planner by integrating a probabilistic scene graph, where object identities and poses are represented as distributions. Building on concepts from CARTO (articulated object uncertainty) and HANDLOOM (1D object tracking), we will use particle filtering or a variational approach to maintain a belief over the scene state. Language grounding will be formulated as inferring the most likely action trajectory given the instruction and the belief state, using a learned cost function that penalizes actions leading to high state entropy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and benchmark for language grounding under perceptual uncertainty. It will yield a robot system capable of handling ambiguous instructions like 'next to the blue cup' when object detection is unreliable, advancing towards robust human-robot interaction.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats perception as a solved input, this integrates continuous uncertainty from low-level vision (like tracking cables or articulated parts) directly into the symbolic reasoning process for language-guided manipulation.

proposals.title: Learning to Execute Multi-Step Manipulation Commands in Dynamic Environments via Language-Conditioned State Abstractions
proposals.motivation: While recent work in robot learning has enabled single-step manipulation of known objects in controlled settings, real-world utility requires executing complex, multi-step natural language commands (e.g., 'untangle the cables and plug in the monitor') in unstructured, dynamic environments. This remains a significant open challenge, as it demands robust perception, long-horizon planning, and adaptation to scene changes, which are not addressed by current single-object or static-scene manipulation frameworks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a robot can robustly execute long-horizon, language-specified tasks in dynamic environments by learning a library of reusable, language-grounded state abstractions from interaction data, enabling it to decompose commands and re-plan based on perceptual updates.
proposals.method: We will extend the state estimation and manipulation principles from HANDLOOM and CARTO to dynamic scenes. The system will use a large language model (fine-tuned with AI feedback methods, as in Kollar's prior work) to parse a command into a sequence of subgoals defined over learned state abstractions (e.g., 'grasped', 'plugged', 'untangled'). A hierarchical policy, trained via reinforcement learning with a time-based curriculum in simulation, will select parameterized skills (like 'fling' for untangling) to achieve each subgoal. A real-time state estimator, fusing RGB-D video and force data, will continuously update these abstractions to enable closed-loop re-planning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a framework for grounding multi-step language commands to a sequence of learned, actionable state representations, 2) empirical results on long-horizon tasks like cable management and articulated object assembly in environments with moving distractors, and 3) an open-source simulated benchmark for dynamic, language-guided manipulation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of language-conditioned, temporally persistent state abstractions for long-horizon task decomposition, combined with reactive re-planning in dynamic settings, moving beyond the single-object, single-action, or static-scene paradigms dominant in pre-2026 robot learning.

proposals.title: VILA-Ground: Video and Language Alignment for Grounding Manipulation Skills from a Single Demonstration
proposals.motivation: Current methods for learning manipulation skills from demonstration, such as those using action sequences or state trajectories, often fail to capture the rich, contextual reasoning behind human actions. This limits a robot's ability to generalize to novel objects or adapt to ambiguous instructions. Grounding actions in multi-modal data, specifically aligning visual dynamics with natural language descriptions, is a critical step toward more interpretable and flexible robot learning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training a grounding model on video demonstrations paired with natural language narration, rather than on action sequences alone, will yield a model that more robustly associates perceptual cues with task semantics, enabling improved generalization to novel objects and states.
proposals.method: We will build upon pre-2026 vision-language models (e.g., CLIP, VideoMAE) and temporal grounding architectures. Our model will encode video demonstrations using a temporal transformer and align these features with paired language instructions via a contrastive loss. For physical grounding, we will integrate this representation with a manipulation policy, using techniques from CARTO for object structure reasoning and HANDLOOM for fine-grained interaction, training the system on a dataset of robot interaction videos with narrated sub-goals.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for learning manipulation skills from video-language pairs, moving beyond pure imitation. It will provide empirical evidence for the value of narrated video data in robotic grounding and release a benchmark dataset of language-annotated manipulation videos to the community.
proposals.novelty: While video-language models exist, their application to grounding low-level robot manipulation skills from a single demonstration is unexplored. The key novelty is the tight integration of temporal video-language alignment with actionable, object-centric representations for physical interaction, distinct from prior work on action segmentation or state-only learning.

proposals.title: Semantic Parsing for Grounded Task Planning: Mapping Natural Language to Formal Actions for Novel Object Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Enabling robots to understand and execute complex natural language instructions for manipulating novel objects remains a core challenge in robotics. Pre-2026, instruction following often relies on rigid templates or end-to-end learning, which struggle with compositional reasoning and generalizing to unseen objects and actions. A formal, interpretable bridge from language to robot action is needed for robust, generalizable task planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that semantic parsing techniques from computational linguistics, which map sentences to formal meaning representations, can be adapted to produce robust, generalizable task plans for robot manipulation, particularly when dealing with novel objects and actions like 'fling' or 'trace'.
proposals.method: We will adapt a neural semantic parser (e.g., a seq2seq model with attention or a grammar-constrained decoder) to map language commands to a formal representation based on Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL) predicates. The parser will be trained on a synthetic corpus of instructions paired with PDDL goal states, generated using language models and grounded in simulation (e.g., PyBullet). We will integrate this with T. Kollar's prior work on object-agnostic reconstruction (CARTO) and manipulation (HANDLOOM) by using the parser's output to initialize planners that reason over reconstructed object state.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, interpretable pipeline for language-conditioned robot planning that explicitly decouples language understanding from plan execution. It will provide a new benchmark for compositional language-to-action tasks and demonstrate improved generalization to novel objects and verbs compared to end-to-end approaches, advancing the field of semantic human-robot interaction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of formal semantic parsing—common in NLP for question answering—to the domain of grounded robotic task planning with novel objects, creating a hybrid symbolic-neural interface that leverages the strengths of both paradigms for improved generalization.

proposals.title: GraspGround: Learning Generalizable Manipulation Priors from Diverse Text Corpora for Novel Object Interaction
proposals.motivation: Robots struggle to interact with novel objects outside their training distribution, requiring extensive data collection for each new item. While large language models (LLMs) encode rich world knowledge about object properties and affordances, this information remains largely untapped for direct, low-level robot control. Grounding this semantic knowledge into actionable manipulation priors is a critical open problem in pre-2026 robot learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-task learning framework, trained on a diverse corpus of robotic interaction data paired with descriptive text, can learn a shared representation that grounds language in physical interaction, enabling zero-shot inference of manipulation strategies for novel objects based solely on their textual description.
proposals.method: We will train a multi-modal transformer encoder on a curated dataset combining existing robotic datasets (e.g., grasping, pushing, flinging) with procedural text (e.g., manuals, wikiHow). The model will be trained with a multi-task objective combining: 1) a contrastive loss aligning visual-object features with textual descriptions, 2) a state-prediction loss for future interaction outcomes (inspired by dynamics models), and 3) a policy distillation loss from successful interaction trajectories. For novel objects, the model will use a text query to condition a learned prior over potential interaction parameters (e.g., grasp points, fling dynamics).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a method for bootstrapping robot manipulation policies from passive text corpora, reducing the need for task-specific physical data. It will provide a benchmark for evaluating language-grounded manipulation priors and demonstrate zero-shot generalization to novel objects in tasks like grasping and non-prehensile manipulation (e.g., flinging).
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that uses language for high-level planning or dataset filtering, we directly learn to condition low-level continuous control parameters on dense textual descriptions of object properties. This bridges the gap between the semantic knowledge in LLMs and the physical interaction models used in works like CARTO and HANDLOOM.

proposals.title: GENCOM: Generating Diverse Natural Language Commands for Robot Manipulation via Dual-Objective LLM Optimization
proposals.motivation: Training robots to understand and execute diverse natural language commands requires large, varied datasets, which are costly and time-consuming to create manually. This data scarcity limits the generalization of vision-language-action models in manipulation tasks, particularly for novel objects or complex instructions. Automatically generating high-quality, instructive command sets can significantly scale up training data and improve robot policy robustness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-objective optimization process for a Large Language Model (LLM), which jointly maximizes linguistic diversity and predicted task-instructiveness, can generate command sets that lead to more performant and generalizable robot manipulation policies than those trained on human-authored or naively augmented data.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage method. First, we fine-tune an LLM (e.g., Llama 2) on existing robot command datasets (e.g., Language-Table, BC-Z) to establish a base command distribution. Second, we employ a reinforcement learning-based objective using a reward function that combines (a) a diversity metric (e.g., embedding variance) and (b) a learned 'instructiveness' scorer trained to predict the success likelihood of a policy given a command, inspired by AI feedback principles. This dual reward will guide the LLM to generate novel, valid, and challenging commands. The generated commands will be paired with simulated robot states (leveraging CARTO-like object representations) to create a large-scale augmented dataset for training a manipulation policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for generating robot training commands via dual-objective LLM optimization, 2) a large, publicly released dataset of diverse natural language commands for manipulation, and 3) empirical evidence showing that training on this generated data improves policy performance on novel objects and complex instructions, especially in low-data regimes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs for data augmentation, our approach uniquely formulates command generation as a dual-objective optimization problem, explicitly trading off diversity against a learned metric of task-instructiveness, rather than relying on simple paraphrasing or template filling.

proposals.title: MINIMAL-GROUND: Single-Word Command Grounding for Time-Critical Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: In time-critical human-robot collaboration, such as disaster response or assistive care, communication must be rapid and robust. Existing methods for language grounding often require full sentences or complex parsing, which introduces latency and can fail under stress or noise. This work investigates the fundamental lower bound of linguistic input needed for effective robot action, addressing a critical gap in real-world HRI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single-word or atomic gesture command, when paired with a learned model of environmental context and object affordances, provides sufficient signal for a robot to execute a correct, goal-directed manipulation sequence in time-critical settings.
proposals.method: We will build on the object-centric representations from CARTO and the 1D tracking from HANDLOOM to create a multi-modal context encoder. A transformer-based grounding model will map a single command (e.g., "Fling," "Pull") and the current scene state to a parameterized action primitive, inspired by prior work on dynamic manipulation like cable flinging. We will train this model using simulated self-supervision and a novel AI feedback mechanism, akin to T. Kollar's prior work on LLM alignment, to refine the grounding policy without dense human annotation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a benchmark for minimal-command robotic grounding under time constraints, 2) a novel architecture for joint language-action grounding that operates on atomic inputs, and 3) empirical evidence defining the sufficiency of single-word commands for a class of dynamic manipulation tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work grounds language to action, it typically assumes phrasal or sentential input. Our focus on the minimal effective unit (single word/gesture) for time-critical scenarios is a distinct and unexplored simplification, moving beyond incremental improvements in parsing to a fundamental study of efficiency in communication for action.

proposals.title: INTEND: INteractive Task Execution with Non-Deterministic Destinations for Ambiguous Object Fetching
proposals.motivation: In real-world human-robot collaboration, instructions like 'bring me the cup' are often ambiguous when multiple candidate objects exist. Pre-2026 robotic fetching and navigation systems typically assume a single, uniquely identifiable goal, limiting their robustness and natural interaction. This work addresses the critical gap of enabling robots to reason over multiple plausible destinations by inferring latent user intent from context, a necessary step for fluid assistive robotics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a robot can effectively resolve ambiguous object-fetching instructions by jointly modeling scene context, user history, and partial observability to infer a probabilistic distribution over user intent, enabling it to act or query for clarification.
proposals.method: We will extend a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) framework where the hidden state is the user's intended target object. The observation model will fuse multi-modal features from a scene graph (inspired by CARTO for object structure) and a learned embedding of the instruction. The policy, trained via online interaction simulation and potential AI feedback (leveraging prior work on LLM alignment for reward shaping), will choose between targeted exploration, disambiguation queries, or direct fetching actions. We will test this in tabletop environments with ambiguous object categories.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a formal POMDP model for ambiguous instruction resolution in manipulation, 2) a novel interactive policy that balances information gathering with task execution, and 3) a benchmark dataset of ambiguous fetching instructions in cluttered scenes to propel future research in intent-aware robotics.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on goal-conditioned policies that assume a single correct destination, this inverts that assumption, explicitly modeling the inherent multiplicity and uncertainty in user goals. It uniquely combines scene-graph reasoning from object reconstruction (CARTO) with interactive decision-making under uncertainty for physical tasks.

proposals.title: DISCOURSE-GROUND: Learning to Ground Complex Linguistic Structures and Discourse for Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current robotic instruction following models (pre-2026) excel at grounding simple, imperative spatial commands but struggle with complex linguistic phenomena like pronouns, coreference, and discourse connectives, which are essential for understanding multi-step natural language instructions. This limits human-robot collaboration in unstructured environments where instructions are often contextual and referential. Building on Kollar's work in object reconstruction (CARTO) and manipulation of deformables (HANDLOOM), this project addresses the gap in grounding higher-level language to physical actions.
proposals.hypothesis: A neuro-symbolic model that explicitly reasons about discourse structure and entity coreference can significantly improve a robot's ability to interpret and execute instructions containing complex linguistic dependencies, compared to end-to-end language-to-action models.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage architecture. First, a fine-tuned transformer (e.g., T5 or a small LLM) will parse instructions into a formal discourse representation structure (DRS), resolving pronouns and connectives. Second, this structured representation will be integrated with a scene graph from CARTO-like object reconstructions and a manipulation policy network. The policy will be trained via reinforcement learning in simulation (e.g., PyBullet) using a curriculum of tasks with increasingly complex language, building on methods for learning from time-series state data and feedback alignment explored in prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel benchmark for robotic instruction following with complex linguistic structures, 2) a publicly released neuro-symbolic architecture for discourse-aware instruction grounding, and 3) empirical validation showing improved task success rates on referential and sequential commands compared to baseline models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that treats language as a flat command, this explicitly models discourse relations and coreference for physical grounding. It uniquely combines formal semantic parsing with learned manipulation policies in a single framework for robotics.

proposals.title: SELF-GROUND: Learning Object Landmark and Relational Models from Egocentric Robot Interaction Sequences
proposals.motivation: Robots operating in novel, unstructured environments require grounded models of objects and their spatial relations to perform complex manipulation. Current methods often rely on large, pre-collected datasets or human supervision, which are brittle and do not scale. Enabling a robot to autonomously build such models from its own continuous perceptual experience is a key step toward lifelong adaptation and learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A robot can learn persistent, reusable grounding models for object landmarks and their relations by integrating multi-view geometric reconstruction from manipulation with temporal state estimation, using its own interaction history as the primary training signal.
proposals.method: We will extend principles from CARTO (category-agnostic reconstruction) and HANDLOOM (1D object tracing) to the problem of learning from egocentric video and proprioception during interaction. A neural field representation will be used to model objects, with keypoints (landmarks) emerging from regions of high local geometric or textural distinctiveness. Temporal graphs, built from robot state and contact estimates (inspired by fling and cable manipulation analysis), will link landmark observations across time. A contrastive learning objective will align visual features of landmarks with their roles in the interaction graph, refining the models without external labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a framework for unsupervised landmark and relation discovery from robot experience, 2) a benchmark for evaluating grounding models in manipulation tasks with novel objects, and 3) empirical evidence that interaction sequences provide a rich signal for structural world modeling.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work on landmark detection (often supervised) or relational learning (often from static scenes), our method jointly learns landmarks and their functional relations directly from a robot's egocentric interaction history, without pre-defined object categories or human annotation.

proposals.title: SEMAP: Learning Semantic Environment Models with Affordance Priors for Robotic Manipulation of Novel Objects
proposals.motivation: Robots operating in unstructured environments must understand not just geometry but object semantics and functional affordances to perform complex tasks. Current semantic mapping systems often provide static category labels but lack actionable, task-oriented representations, limiting a robot's ability to interact with novel object instances. This gap is critical for applications like domestic assistance or logistics, where robots encounter diverse, unseen objects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating category-agnostic articulated object reconstruction (like CARTO) with learned affordance models into a unified semantic map will enable a robot to successfully infer manipulation strategies for novel objects from a single interaction or observation.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical mapping system. A volumetric semantic map (e.g., using a neural implicit representation like NICE-SLAM) will fuse geometric and categorical data from an RGB-D stream. This map will be linked to an instance-level graph where each object node is enriched with reconstructions from CARTO (for pose and articulation) and affordance predictions inspired by HANDLOOM's tracing of 1D structures. We will train an affordance prior network on interaction data (e.g., from 'fling' or cable manipulation primitives) to propose likely interaction points and parameters (e.g., pull, grasp) based on object shape and category, enabling planning for novel objects.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel environment representation unifying geometry, semantics, articulation, and affordances, 2) a method for inferring manipulation strategies for novel objects by leveraging categorical priors and geometric similarity, and 3) empirical validation on robotic tasks involving novel articulated and deformable objects.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight coupling of a metric semantic map with instance-level, category-agnostic articulated models and a learned affordance prior, moving beyond static labeling to a dynamic, interaction-ready world model for novel objects.

proposals.title: LINGO-NAV: Learning End-to-End Navigation from Natural Language Demonstrations via Imitation and Inverse Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Endowing robots with the ability to follow natural language directions in novel, unstructured environments remains a core challenge in embodied AI. Pre-2026, most approaches rely on modular pipelines (parsing, mapping, planning) that are brittle and struggle with perceptual ambiguity and unseen objects. An end-to-end policy that directly maps language and perception to low-level navigation actions could enable more robust and flexible instruction following.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a policy trained via imitation learning (IL) on human demonstrations, refined with inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) to recover underlying navigational intent, can achieve more robust and generalizable language-conditioned navigation in novel environments than current modular or purely IL-based methods.
proposals.method: We will collect a dataset of human teleoperation demonstrations paired with natural language commands in simulated environments (e.g., Habitat, iGibson). We will train an initial behavioral cloning (BC) policy using a transformer-based architecture that fuses encoded language instructions and egocentric visual observations (from a ResNet backbone) to output continuous navigation velocities. To address BC's compounding error and recover the demonstrator's latent reward function, we will refine the policy using adversarial inverse reinforcement learning (AIRL), treating the demonstrations as expert trajectories. The state representation will be augmented with geometric and semantic features informed by Kollar's prior work on object reconstruction (CARTO) to better handle novel objects.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel IL+IRL framework for end-to-end language-guided navigation, 2) a public dataset of language-annotated navigation demonstrations in photorealistic simulators, and 3) empirical evidence showing that recovering intent via IRL improves generalization over pure imitation, particularly for commands involving references to novel objects.
proposals.novelty: While IL has been used for navigation, its combination with IRL for language-conditioned tasks is less explored. The key novelty is the integration of an IRL objective specifically to disentangle and learn the navigational intent embedded in language commands, moving beyond direct action mimicry to infer the underlying spatial goals, especially for instructions about objects not seen during training.

proposals.title: LSP-Ground: Grounding Language for Physical Instruction Following via Learned Scene Perturbations
proposals.motivation: Robots that can follow open-ended physical instructions (e.g., 'fling the cable over the bar') require robust grounding of language into perception and dynamics models. Pre-2026, methods often rely on large, static datasets or explicit 3D models, struggling with novel objects and deformable materials. This limits real-world applicability where object properties and scene states are uncertain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a Learned Scene Perturbation (LSP) model, trained to predict the perceptual consequences of actions described in language, can provide a more robust and generalizable grounding for physical instruction following than direct state-estimation or end-to-end policy learning.
proposals.method: We will extend the LSP framework from prior work on 1D objects to articulated and novel rigid objects. The system will consist of: 1) a perception module leveraging CARTO for category-agnostic articulated object reconstruction and HANDLOOM for deformable object tracing; 2) an LSP model that, given a language instruction and initial scene observation, predicts a distribution over post-action perceptual changes (e.g., keypoint displacements); 3) a planner that uses these predictions to select actions (like 'fling') that maximize the likelihood of achieving the instructed goal state, evaluated through a learned reward function.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel paradigm for language grounding that bridges high-level instructions and low-level robot control through learned perceptual dynamics. It will provide a benchmark and methodology for evaluating instruction following with novel objects, specifically in tasks involving articulation and deformation, advancing beyond current state-based or purely end-to-end approaches.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing LSP—previously used for inspection and reconstruction—as a core component for action-oriented language grounding, and in integrating it with state-of-the-art agnostic reconstruction (CARTO) and tracing (HANDLOOM) methods to handle a diverse set of novel objects in physical tasks.

proposals.title: SEARCH-RL: Reinforcement Learning for Efficient Visual Search of Novel Objects in Large-Scale Cluttered Scenes
proposals.motivation: Robots operating in warehouses or homes must find specific objects in large, cluttered scenes, a task known as visual search. Classical methods, like exhaustive scanning, are inefficient, while learning-based methods often require extensive data per object. An efficient, generalizable search policy for novel objects, based on minimal prior information, remains an open challenge crucial for practical robot autonomy.
proposals.hypothesis: A deep reinforcement learning agent, trained on a diverse set of simulated scenes and objects, can learn a general search policy that efficiently localizes novel object referents by actively selecting informative viewpoints, outperforming systematic search baselines.
proposals.method: We will frame active visual search as a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POVMP). The agent's state is a history of egocentric RGB-D observations, and actions parameterize camera motions (e.g., pan, tilt, small translation). Rewards will be sparse (+1 for correct localization, -0.01 per step). We will use Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) as our core RL algorithm for its sample efficiency. Training will occur in a photo-realistic simulator (e.g., SAPIEN, iGibson) using a large dataset of articulated and novel objects, building on CARTO and HANDLOOM's object representations to generate varied training scenes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a learned, general-purpose search policy that transfers to novel objects without retraining, 2) a benchmark for active visual search in large-scale simulated environments, and 3) an analysis of how object properties (e.g., articulation, texture) influence learned search behavior.
proposals.novelty: While RL has been used for object finding, our approach uniquely focuses on sample-efficient learning for *novel* object referents in expansive scenes, tightly integrating recent advances in articulated object modeling (CARTO) and 1D object tracing (HANDLOOM) to create a rich, structured training domain.

proposals.title: PARSE-MTL: Multi-Task Learning for Joint Parsing and Perception of Deformable Linear Objects
proposals.motivation: Robotic manipulation of deformable one-dimensional objects (e.g., cables, ropes) is crucial for tasks in manufacturing and domestic assistance, yet remains a significant challenge. Existing methods often treat perception (state estimation) and parsing (understanding topological structure) as separate problems, leading to brittle systems that struggle with complex configurations and occlusions. A unified framework that leverages shared representations could dramatically improve robustness and sample efficiency for learning-based manipulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-task learning framework, jointly trained on auxiliary tasks like semantic segmentation and keypoint detection, will produce a more robust and generalizable representation for the state estimation and topological parsing of deformable linear objects than single-task models.
proposals.method: We will build upon the HANDLOOM architecture for 1D object tracing and CARTO's principles for agnostic reconstruction. The core model will be a shared encoder (e.g., a ResNet or Transformer backbone) processing RGB-D input, with multiple decoder heads for the primary tasks of 3D state reconstruction and contact point parsing, and auxiliary tasks of pixel-wise semantic segmentation and 2D/3D keypoint prediction. We will employ gradient balancing techniques like Uncertainty Weighting to manage task conflicts. Training will use a combination of simulated data (from PyBullet or NVIDIA Isaac Sim) and real-world data collected with robotic flinging and manipulation setups.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, unified neural network architecture for deformable linear object manipulation that jointly reasons about geometry and topology. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of multi-task learning for representation learning in robotic perception, specifically improving performance in low-data regimes and under occlusion. The resulting model and dataset will be released to the community.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning is common in vision, its application to the joint problem of 3D state estimation and topological parsing for highly deformable objects is novel. This approach uniquely bridges the gap between the geometric reconstruction focus of HANDLOOM and the structural understanding needed for complex manipulation, moving beyond single-task or sequential pipelines.

proposals.title: DUAL: Learning Deformable Object Manipulation Policies from Language-Guided Visual Pre-Training
proposals.motivation: Robotic manipulation of deformable one-dimensional objects (e.g., cables, ropes) remains a significant challenge due to complex state representation and dynamics. Pre-2026, methods like T. Kollar's HANDLOOM enable state estimation, but learning robust manipulation policies often requires extensive, hard-to-collect physical interaction data. Leveraging the abundance of web-scale image-text pairs offers a promising, scalable source of supervision for learning visual features relevant to deformable object structure and affordances.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that visual representations pre-trained using natural language descriptions of object states and interactions will enable more sample-efficient learning of downstream robotic manipulation policies for deformable objects, compared to representations trained solely on standard image classification or self-supervised objectives.
proposals.method: We will extend a vision-language model (e.g., CLIP) pre-training paradigm. We will curate a dataset of images depicting cables in various configurations, paired with synthetic language statements describing physical properties (e.g., 'The rope is tightly coiled,' 'The cable is draped over the hook'). A contrastive loss will align these images and texts in a shared embedding space. This pre-trained encoder will then be frozen and used to extract features for a downstream policy network (e.g., a Transformer or CNN-LSTM) trained via reinforcement learning in simulation on tasks like cable routing or flinging, using the state estimation techniques from HANDLOOM as a partial observation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a method for bootstrapping robotic visual representations from passive image-text data, reducing reliance on interaction-heavy pre-training; 2) an empirical evaluation of how language-guided features impact policy learning efficiency and generalization for deformable object manipulation; 3) a new simulated benchmark combining language descriptions with cable manipulation tasks.
proposals.novelty: While vision-language models are established, their application for pre-training representations specifically for deformable object manipulation, and the systematic study of how descriptive language about physical state transfers to control policy learning, is novel. This bridges the gap between large-scale passive pre-training and fine-grained physical reasoning for robotics.

proposals.title: EXPLAIN: EXplicit Probabilistic Language for Action INterpretation in Robot Manipulation
proposals.motivation: As robots move into unstructured environments, their ability to handle novel objects and uncertain state estimates is critical. Current methods often treat the human as a questioner to resolve ambiguity, but this is inefficient and can break task flow. Proactive explanation by the robot can build trust, enable faster collaborative problem-solving, and improve task success in single-shot or learning-from-demonstration scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: A robot that proactively generates natural language explanations of its perceived uncertainty and planned actions, grounded in its internal probabilistic state and manipulation models, will lead to more efficient human-robot collaboration and higher perceived trust compared to reactive query-based systems.
proposals.method: We will build on the probabilistic articulation models from CARTO and the 1D object tracking from HANDLOOM to create a structured, factor-graph-based representation of task uncertainty (e.g., object pose, articulation state, cable dynamics). This representation will be linked to a conditioned language model (e.g., fine-tuned LLM) to generate concise explanations (e.g., "I'm flinging the rope because I'm uncertain about the knot's topology"). We will evaluate this in a novel object manipulation testbed involving tasks with cables and articulated objects, comparing EXPLAIN against a baseline where the robot only answers questions.
proposals.contribution: 1) A framework for translating robot internal state (probabilistic models, manipulation affordances) into natural language explanations. 2) Empirical evidence on how proactive explanation affects collaboration efficiency and trust in physical manipulation tasks. 3) A public dataset of robot actions paired with uncertainty metrics and human-preferred explanations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses language for querying or instruction following, this inverts the interaction: the robot acts as an explainer, initiating communication based on its own uncertainty models. It uniquely bridges low-level manipulation research (like flinging or tracing) with high-level, human-centric communication.

proposals.title: Temporal Reasoning for Robotic Manipulation: Learning to Infer Object State Changes from Partial Observations
proposals.motivation: Robots operating in human environments must execute tasks where object states can change between command issuance and execution, due to external agents or dynamics. Existing methods, like those for reconstructing articulated objects (CARTO) or tracing deformable ones (HANDLOOM), typically assume static scenes or full observability, limiting real-world applicability. This work addresses the critical gap of reasoning about unobserved state transitions in dynamic settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model trained to jointly reconstruct object state and infer likely temporal transitions from sparse, partial observations can significantly improve a robot's ability to plan and act in environments where objects have moved or changed configuration.
proposals.method: We will extend the category-agnostic reconstruction framework of CARTO with a temporal prediction module. The system will take as input a partial point cloud (pre- and post-change) and a natural language command. A transformer-based architecture will encode these inputs, leveraging pre-2026 large language models for command understanding. A latent dynamics model, inspired by pre-2026 neural scene representation works, will learn to predict the most probable state change (e.g., articulation, translation) that occurred, conditioned on the command and object geometry. The output will be a reconstructed 3D model of the object in its new state, enabling subsequent manipulation planning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel benchmark for evaluating robotic temporal reasoning with dynamic objects, and 2) an integrated architecture for state inference and reconstruction that relaxes the static-world assumption common in pre-2026 manipulation research. It directly extends the researcher's work on novel object understanding into temporal domains.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight coupling of category-agnostic 3D reconstruction with learned, command-conditioned dynamics prediction, moving beyond static scene analysis or purely observational forecasting to task-relevant state inference for manipulation.

proposals.title: PROB-VL: Probabilistic Grounding with Rich Object-Centric and Relational Features for Novel Object Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Robots operating in unstructured environments must ground human instructions to novel objects, a core challenge in learning-based manipulation. Existing probabilistic grounding models, such as those in Kollar's prior work on cables and articulated objects, often rely on limited geometric or categorical features, failing to capture the rich visual attributes (e.g., texture, fine-grained parts) and complex relational language (e.g., 'the frayed end of the striped rope') needed for precise task execution. This limits a robot's ability to disambiguate and interact with never-before-seen objects from single or few demonstrations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting a probabilistic grounding model with object-centric visual representations from pre-trained vision models (e.g., DINOv2, SAM) and a broad vocabulary of attribute and relational predicates extracted from a large language model will significantly improve the accuracy and sample efficiency of learning manipulation policies for novel objects from limited data.
proposals.method: We will extend a probabilistic grounding framework (e.g., building on CARTO's structure) by integrating a multi-stream visual encoder. This encoder will extract dense, pre-trained features for object parts and material properties, alongside geometric state. A frozen, instruction-tuned LLM (e.g., Flan-T5) will parse natural language commands into a structured set of attribute and relational constraints (e.g., 'grasp <texture:rubbery> <part:handle>'). A neural graph network will then perform probabilistic inference over the object state and these constraints to predict manipulation parameters, trained via a variational objective on demonstration data featuring novel objects.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, modular architecture for integrating rich, pre-trained visual and linguistic features into robot learning, 2) a public benchmark for novel object instruction grounding featuring complex attributes and relations, and 3) empirical validation showing improved generalization in manipulation tasks on deformable and articulated objects compared to feature-poor baselines.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs for high-level planning or simple keywords, our approach uniquely integrates them for fine-grained, probabilistic attribute and relation extraction directly into a low-level grounding and control loop, tightly coupling dense visual features with a broad linguistic ontology for physical interaction.

proposals.title: DialLOOM: Language-Guided Manipulation of Deformable Linear Objects with Minimalist Robot Sensing
proposals.motivation: Deploying robots in unstructured environments like homes requires low-cost, minimalist platforms with poor sensing (e.g., no depth cameras, limited proprioception). Existing methods for deformable object manipulation, like those for cables, rely heavily on rich state estimation. This work addresses the gap where robots must perform precise tasks, such as tracing or knotting, with severely degraded perception, necessitating a shift towards interactive, language-mediated strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dialog-driven policy, which uses natural language to query a human for sparse, corrective feedback and to ground ambiguous sensory data, can enable effective one-dimensional object manipulation on robots with minimal onboard sensing.
