proposals.method: We will extend the HANDLOOM framework, which learns to trace 1D objects, by replacing its precise state estimator with a low-dimensional, uncertain belief state from poor sensors (e.g., binary contact sensors). A hierarchical policy will be trained via simulation and reinforcement learning: a high-level dialog manager, informed by methods for AI feedback alignment, will generate language queries to resolve state ambiguity, while a low-level controller executes primitives like 'fling' or 'pull' based on the clarified belief. The system will be evaluated on real cable manipulation tasks using a robot with deliberately impoverished sensing.
proposals.contribution: This will contribute a novel framework for human-robot dialog grounded in manipulation under severe perceptual constraints. It will demonstrate how language can compensate for missing sensor modalities, advancing towards more affordable and adaptable robots. The work will also provide a benchmark for language-assisted manipulation with minimalist hardware.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses language for high-level planning or rich sensory grounding, our approach uniquely focuses on dialog as a critical, dynamic component of the state estimation and control loop for deformable objects when robot perception is fundamentally inadequate.

proposals.title: Learning to Be Understood: Modeling Human Adaptation of Referential Language to Robot Perceptual and Physical Limitations
proposals.motivation: Human-robot interaction (HRI) research has focused on making robots understand human language, but the inverse problem—how humans adapt their language when they perceive a robot's limitations—is understudied. This adaptation is crucial for efficient collaboration, especially with robots that have non-anthropomorphic perception (e.g., from CARTO) or are tasked with manipulating novel, deformable objects (e.g., cables as in HANDLOOM). Understanding this process can inform better robot design and communication protocols.
proposals.hypothesis: Humans will systematically simplify and structuralize their referential language, moving from feature-based to action- and state-based descriptions, when they infer that a robot has limitations in visual perception or physical manipulation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled HRI study where participants teach a robot to manipulate novel, deformable objects (e.g., bundled cables) via language. The robot's perceived limitations will be manipulated between subjects (e.g., 'poor vision' vs. 'dexterous'). We will record and transcribe instructions, then analyze linguistic features (syntactic complexity, word choice, discourse structure). We will model the adaptation process using a probabilistic graphical model (inspired by inverse reinforcement learning) that infers the human's latent belief about the robot's capabilities from the language stream, linking it to the robot's internal state and time-series action data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a computational model of human linguistic adaptation to robot limitations, 2) a novel dataset of human-robot dialogue under constrained perception/action, and 3) practical insights for designing robots that can signal their capabilities to elicit more effective human instruction.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (e.g., on AI feedback for LLMs) studies how machines learn from human input, we invert the perspective to model how humans learn to communicate with machines. This directly connects Kollar's expertise in reconstructing object state (CARTO) and tracing deformables (HANDLOOM) to the core HRI problem of bidirectional communication.

proposals.title: Learning Grounded Models of Complex Motion Verbs and Spatial Prepositions for One-Dimensional Object Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, robots struggle with natural language instructions involving complex spatial and dynamic concepts. While prior work has modeled basic verbs like 'follow' and prepositions like 'to', enabling robots to understand and execute commands like 'wrap the cable around the post' or 'thread the rope through the loop' remains an open challenge. This limits the utility of robots in unstructured environments where tasks involve deformable, one-dimensional objects (e.g., cables, ropes, threads) and require nuanced spatial reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by combining category-agnostic articulated object reconstruction (CARTO) with a temporal state-space model of manipulation, we can learn grounded, executable models for a richer lexicon of verbs (e.g., 'coil', 'thread', 'drape') and spatial prepositions (e.g., 'around', 'through', 'under') from a single or few demonstrations.
proposals.method: We will extend the HANDLOOM framework for tracing 1D objects to incorporate a learned dynamics model over the object's state (shape, pose) and robot actions. Using CARTO's reconstruction as a state representation, we will train a transformer-based temporal model to predict future object states given action sequences. We will collect a dataset of human demonstrations paired with language labels for target verbs/prepositions. The model will learn to map language to a latent goal state distribution and generate action sequences via model-predictive control to achieve it, using the learned dynamics for planning.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a dataset and benchmark for complex verb/preposition grounding with 1D objects, 2) a unified neural model that integrates reconstruction, dynamics, and language for task execution, and 3) an empirical evaluation showing generalization to novel objects and spatial configurations. This moves beyond static spatial relations to model dynamic, contact-rich interactions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in jointly learning dynamics and language grounding for deformable linear object manipulation, a domain where spatial relations are highly state-dependent and non-rigid. Unlike prior work on rigid objects or simple prepositions, this addresses the temporal evolution of spatial concepts like 'around' during a 'coiling' action.

proposals.title: DECOMPOSE: Learning to Parse and Execute Complex Multi-Task Commands for Robotic Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Current robotic instruction-following systems excel at single, well-defined tasks but struggle with complex natural language commands involving sequential, concurrent, or conditional sub-tasks (e.g., 'fling the towel over the rack, then wipe the table while holding the sponge'). This limits their utility in unstructured environments like homes or warehouses. Enabling robots to understand and decompose such intricate commands is a critical step toward more capable and autonomous assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neuro-symbolic framework, combining a large language model for semantic parsing with a learned, object-centric task graph representation, can robustly decompose complex commands into executable primitive actions, outperforming end-to-end language-to-action models on compositional tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage system. First, a fine-tuned LLM (e.g., Codex or Code Llama) will parse a command into a structured task graph, representing temporal (sequential/concurrent) and object-centric dependencies, inspired by the object-aware reconstruction in CARTO. Second, a learned policy network, conditioned on this graph and the robot's state, will execute the plan. This policy will leverage pre-trained visual representations and manipulation primitives (like the flinging or cable-tracing skills from prior work) as low-level actions. We will train and evaluate the system on a novel simulation benchmark featuring complex commands over articulated and deformable objects.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel benchmark for evaluating compositional robotic instruction following, 2) a publicly released neuro-symbolic architecture for command decomposition and execution, and 3) empirical evidence on the efficacy of combining LLM-based parsing with learned, object-centric task graphs for long-horizon manipulation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of LLM-based symbolic reasoning for command parsing with a learned, grounded execution policy that reasons about object state over time, moving beyond treating commands as monolithic or using rigid, hand-coded task grammars.

proposals.title: LANGROB: Language-Grounded Task Execution via Robust Human Tracking and Scene Landmark Recognition
proposals.motivation: For robots to follow natural language instructions in dynamic, human-populated environments (e.g., 'hand me the tool near the person by the cabinet'), they must ground language to the physical state of people and objects. Pre-2026, language grounding systems often assume static scenes or perfect perception, while robust tracking and recognition modules operate without semantic task context. This gap limits reliable human-robot collaboration.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a language grounding model with robust, real-time person tracking and landmark recognition systems will enable a robot to interpret and execute spatial language commands with significantly higher success rates in cluttered, dynamic environments compared to systems lacking this integrated perception.
proposals.method: We will build on the language grounding principles from CARTO and HANDLOOM, and integrate them with state-of-the-art pre-2026 perception modules: a real-time multi-person tracker (e.g., using CenterTrack or FairMOT) and a landmark recognition system (e.g., using SuperPoint/SuperGlue or Dense Object Nets). A central multimodal state estimator will fuse the tracker's 3D person poses, the recognizer's landmark coordinates, and the robot's proprioception. This unified spatiotemporal state will be the input to a transformer-based language grounding model, trained to map phrases like 'near the person' to geometric constraints relative to the tracked state.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a unified architecture for dynamic language grounding, 2) a novel benchmark for evaluating language-guided manipulation in human-present scenes, and 3) empirical validation showing the necessity of tight perception-language coupling for robustness.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop integration where the language model's interpretative priors actively guide the attention and association mechanisms of the perception systems (tracking, recognition), moving beyond a simple pipeline where perception runs independently.

proposals.title: Phrase-Based Translation of Natural Language Commands to Planning Domain Definitions for Novel Object Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Enabling robots to understand open-ended natural language commands for manipulating novel objects remains a core challenge. Current methods often rely on pre-defined semantic parsing or large, task-specific datasets, which lack flexibility. A robust, data-driven translation from language to formal planning language could allow robots to interpret a wider variety of instructions for tasks like flinging or untangling cables with novel objects.
proposals.hypothesis: Phrase-based statistical machine translation (SMT) models, trained on aligned corpora of natural language commands and Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL) fragments, can accurately generate executable planning problem definitions for robotic manipulation tasks involving novel objects.
proposals.method: We will create a parallel corpus by annotating natural language commands from existing robotics instruction datasets (e.g., from prior 'fling' or 'cable' manipulation work) with corresponding PDDL problem files. Using the Moses SMT toolkit, we will train a phrase-based translation model with a log-linear combination of features (phrase translation, lexical weighting, distortion, language model). The target 'language' will be a templated subset of PDDL. We will evaluate translation accuracy via BLEU score against ground-truth PDDL and by executing the translated output in a simulated planner and state estimator, similar to those used in CARTO and HANDLOOM.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, data-driven pipeline for grounding language in action planning, reducing the need for hand-crafted semantic parsers. It will also provide a publicly available parallel corpus of language-PDDL pairs for robotic manipulation tasks, facilitating future research in this intersection.
proposals.novelty: While SMT is mature for natural languages, its application to translate directly into a structured planning language for robotics is novel. This approach leverages the robustness and alignment capabilities of SMT to handle the compositional structure of both language and planning problems, differing from contemporary end-to-end neural approaches that lack explicit symbolic grounding.

proposals.title: LINGO-FLING: Learning Language-Conditioned Dynamic Manipulation Policies via Reinforcement Learning and Self-Supervision
proposals.motivation: Enabling robots to manipulate novel objects based on open-ended language instructions (e.g., 'fling the cable to untangle it') remains a significant challenge. Pre-2026, methods often rely on explicit state estimation and motion planning, which can be brittle for deformable or articulated objects. A direct, trial-and-error policy that maps language and state to low-level actions could enable more adaptive and dynamic behaviors.
proposals.hypothesis: A reinforcement learning agent, trained in simulation with a dense, self-supervised reward signal derived from interaction outcomes, can learn a robust policy that directly maps language embeddings and proprioceptive state to dynamic manipulation actions for novel objects.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation environment in Isaac Gym or MuJoCo featuring diverse one-dimensional (cables) and articulated objects. The policy will be a deep neural network (e.g., Transformer or RNN) taking language embeddings (from a frozen pre-2026 model like CLIP or BERT) and robot state as input, outputting action parameters for dynamic primitives like 'fling'. We will use an on-policy RL algorithm (PPO or SAC) with a carefully shaped reward function combining task success (e.g., untangling length) and motion efficiency. Techniques from CARTO and HANDLOOM will inform state representation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a framework for learning direct language-to-action policies for dynamic manipulation, 2) empirical evidence on the sample efficiency and generalization of such policies to novel objects, and 3) a publicly available simulation benchmark for language-conditioned dynamic manipulation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses RL for manipulation or language for task specification, combining them for direct control in dynamic, object-agnostic tasks (inspired by 'fling' and 'cables' manipulation) is less explored. The key novelty is the tight integration of language conditioning with RL for policies that must infer physical object properties from interaction.

proposals.title: MAGIC: Multi-Agent Grounding of Implicit Coordination Commands
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, robots that understand natural language commands are typically single-agent, focusing on individual manipulation or navigation. Real-world tasks like 'form a line' or 'meet at the door' require multiple robots to interpret a single command and infer their distinct, coordinated roles. Scaling language grounding to this multi-agent, implicit coordination setting is a critical open problem for effective human-robot teaming.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a centralized grounding model, trained on simulated multi-robot interactions and leveraging object-centric scene representations, can decompose a global natural language command into individual robot action sequences that achieve implicit spatial coordination.
proposals.method: We will extend single-agent grounding architectures (e.g., CLIPort-style models) to a multi-agent setting. A central transformer-based network will process a fused input: the command, a global scene representation (from multiple robot cameras), and learned embeddings for each robot. Inspired by CARTO's object-agnostic approach, we will use a 3D spatial feature field as the scene representation. The model will output a sequence of goal states or primitive actions (e.g., 'fling', 'move-to') for each robot. Training will use simulated data where commands automatically generate coordinated trajectories, with a reward shaping penalty for failed coordination.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel dataset and benchmark for multi-robot command grounding with implicit coordination, 2) a neural architecture for centralized command decomposition, and 3) an empirical analysis of how scene representation fidelity impacts coordination success in grounded commands.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior multi-robot work on explicit task allocation or swarm behaviors, this focuses on grounding a single, high-level natural language command into decentralized actions requiring implicit role assignment and spatial reasoning, merging language grounding with multi-agent planning.

proposals.title: LEAD: Learning Expressive Action Costs from Human Demonstrations for Deformable Linear Object Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Robustly manipulating deformable one-dimensional objects (e.g., cables, ropes) remains a significant challenge in robotics, as their high-dimensional, non-linear state dynamics make traditional planning and control difficult. While learning from demonstration (LfD) is promising, most pre-2026 methods for deformables learn direct policy imitation, which lacks the generalizability and interpretability needed for novel objects and varied commands. Inverse Optimal Control (IOC) can infer a cost function explaining human preferences, but has been under-explored for complex, high-DoF deformable object tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an IOC framework, trained on pairs of natural language commands and human trajectory data for deformable linear object (DLO) manipulation, can learn a cost model that generalizes to novel DLOs and unseen commands better than behavioral cloning, enabling more robust and interpretable robot execution.
proposals.method: We will collect a dataset of human demonstrations performing tasks (e.g., untangling, shaping) on various DLOs, each paired with a natural language command. Building on pre-2026 IOC methods like Maximum Entropy Deep Inverse Reinforcement Learning, we will train a neural network to map from a fused representation of the object state (inspired by HANDLOOM's tracing) and a command embedding (from a pre-trained language model) to a cost function. This cost function will then be used within a model-predictive control planner, leveraging dynamics models informed by prior work on DLO state estimation, to generate robot trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel dataset of language-annotated human demonstrations for DLO manipulation, 2) a learned cost model that provides an interpretable representation of task objectives for deformables, and 3) empirical validation showing improved generalization to novel objects and commands over end-to-end imitation learning baselines, advancing toward more adaptable deformable object manipulation.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of language-conditioned IOC specifically for the domain of deformable linear objects, moving beyond direct policy learning to infer reusable cost structures, and leveraging recent advances in DLO state representation (HANDLOOM) and category-agnostic understanding (CARTO) for generalization.

proposals.title: VERB-VISE: Grounding Manipulation Commands in Abstract Task Spaces via Learned One-Dimensional State Representations
proposals.motivation: Robots that can follow abstract, verb-based commands (e.g., 'fling the cable', 'stretch the cloth') in unstructured environments are crucial for general-purpose assistive robotics. Prior work on verb grounding has focused largely on navigation, leaving a gap for dexterous manipulation tasks where object state is complex and often low-dimensional. Grounding commands for novel object manipulation requires linking language to learned, actionable representations of object state and dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by representing manipulation tasks and object states within learned, low-dimensional abstract spaces—inspired by 1D representations for deformable objects—a robot can ground and execute verb-based commands for novel objects more effectively than with raw perception or fixed state representations.
proposals.method: We will extend the verb grounding framework to manipulation by leveraging methods from HANDLOOM and CARTO. First, we will learn category-agnostic, 1D state representations for articulated and deformable objects from visual input, capturing task-relevant geometry (e.g., cable curvature, joint angles). Second, we will map natural language verbs (e.g., 'untangle', 'flatten') to trajectories within this abstract state space using a learned model. Finally, we will use model-predictive control to generate robot actions that drive the object's state along the commanded trajectory, evaluating on tasks like cable shaping and articulated object reconfiguration.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for grounding manipulation verbs in learned abstract task spaces, 2) a publicly available dataset of language-annotated manipulation sequences with varied objects, and 3) empirical validation on real-robot tasks demonstrating improved generalization to novel objects compared to end-to-end visuolinguistic policies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of verb grounding—previously applied to navigation—with category-agnostic, low-dimensional state reconstruction for manipulation, creating a principled bridge between abstract language and the physical dynamics of novel objects.

proposals.title: CLOSELOOP-SEARCH: Closing the Perception-Action Loop for Probabilistic Object Search via Real-Time Visual Grounding
proposals.motivation: Robotic search for novel objects in unstructured environments remains a significant challenge. Existing probabilistic search models, like POMDPs, often rely on static, pre-computed object detectors or unrealistic observation models, limiting their robustness to perceptual aliasing and novel object instances encountered during execution. Integrating real-time, adaptive visual perception directly into the search loop is a critical step toward deployable systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that grounding a probabilistic search policy with a real-time, continually updated object detection stream will significantly improve search efficiency and success rates for novel objects by dynamically reducing state uncertainty and correcting belief drift.
proposals.method: We will extend a POMDP-based search framework with a perception module built on a fast, fine-tunable object detector (e.g., YOLOv7 or a EfficientDet variant). During execution, the robot's path and camera feed will provide new image patches. These will be processed by the detector, whose outputs (bounding boxes and class probabilities) will be converted into observation likelihoods for the POMDP belief update. Inspired by HANDLOOM's tracing and CARTO's reconstruction, we will treat partial, sequential detections as evidence for the object's presence and state, dynamically pruning the search tree. The system will be evaluated in simulation and on a physical robot platform.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a unified architecture for tight coupling of real-time vision and decision-theoretic search, 2) a publicly available benchmark for closed-loop visual search, and 3) empirical validation showing quantifiable gains in time-to-find and success rate over open-loop baselines.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the components individually, but in their closed-loop integration: using the *execution path itself* as an active perception strategy to continuously ground and refine the search belief, moving beyond static world assumptions common in pre-2026 search literature.

proposals.title: SEMANTIC-PROB: Integrating LLM Priors for Probabilistic State Estimation in Deformable and Articulated Object Manipulation
proposals.motivation: Robotic manipulation of novel, non-rigid, and articulated objects (e.g., cables, textiles, unknown tools) remains a significant challenge. State estimation for such objects is often under-constrained, relying heavily on geometric models or learned visual features that lack functional and physical commonsense. Pre-2026, large language models (LLMs) and vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated rich semantic and physical knowledge, but this has not been systematically integrated into the probabilistic models that drive robot perception and planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating semantic and physical priors from large language/vision-language models as factors in a robot's probabilistic graphical model will significantly improve state estimation accuracy and sample efficiency for manipulating novel deformable and articulated objects.
proposals.method: We will extend probabilistic frameworks used in prior work (e.g., factor graphs in CARTO for articulation, particle filters for deformables in HANDLOOM) by introducing novel semantic factor nodes. These nodes will be powered by frozen, pre-trained VLMs (e.g., CLIP, PaLI-X) and LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, Codex). For a given observation (image, point cloud), the VLM will generate likelihoods over object categories, parts, and potential actions (e.g., 'fling', 'pull'), while the LLM will infer plausible physical constraints and articulation hierarchies. These outputs will be formatted as soft constraints or likelihood functions within the factor graph, guiding the inference of latent object state (pose, shape, articulation) over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, hybrid probabilistic architecture that fuses geometric perception with semantic world knowledge from foundation models, 2) quantitative evidence showing improved state estimation for novel object manipulation tasks, and 3) a public benchmark and dataset focusing on semantically-rich manipulation scenarios with deformable and articulated objects.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses LLMs/VLMs for high-level task planning or language conditioning, our approach is novel in directly embedding their knowledge as differentiable, probabilistic factors within low-level state estimation models, creating a tighter coupling between semantics and geometry for manipulation.

proposals.title: CABLE: Context-Aware Bayesian Learning for Efficient Multi-Robot Object Search
proposals.motivation: In unstructured environments like homes or warehouses, robots must find novel objects based on incomplete descriptions. Single-robot search is often inefficient, while existing multi-robot coordination methods typically lack the ability to incorporate high-level semantic context to guide the search, leading to wasted time and energy. This problem is critical for practical applications in logistics and domestic assistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-robot search system, which integrates learned object-context relationships (e.g., a 'hammer' is likely in a 'workshop') into a decentralized coordination framework, will significantly reduce the time and state-space coverage required to locate a novel target object compared to context-agnostic or single-robot baselines.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-tier probabilistic framework. First, a context module, building on the category-agnostic shape priors from CARTO, will learn from prior scenes to generate a spatial probability distribution over likely object locations given a semantic query. Second, this distribution will initialize a decentralized, communication-aware variant of Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) for multi-robot coordination, where each robot plans paths to maximize collective information gain, inspired by information-theoretic exploration but constrained by real-world factors like occlusion and the need to manipulate clutter (extending HANDLOOM's tracing for verification).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel integration method for semantic context into probabilistic multi-robot search, 2) a decentralized MCTS algorithm for communication-limited coordination in object search tasks, and 3) a public benchmark for context-aware multi-robot search in simulated and real cluttered environments.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing learned, category-agnostic object-context models (from single-object manipulation research) with decentralized multi-robot decision-making, creating a system that reasons about 'what' it is looking for to decide 'where' and 'how' to look, a step beyond purely geometric coverage or known-object search.

proposals.title: DYNAMOR: Dynamic World Model Updates for Robotic Manipulation of Novel, Deformable Objects
proposals.motivation: Robots operating in unstructured environments, such as homes, must interact with novel objects whose state and physical properties are not pre-defined. Static world models fail for non-rigid objects like cables or textiles, where the state changes dramatically during manipulation (e.g., via flinging). This necessitates a perception-driven, continuously updating representation to enable robust planning and control.
proposals.hypothesis: A world model that dynamically updates its geometric and physical state representation from a single, ongoing perceptual stream will enable a robot to successfully complete complex manipulation tasks with novel, deformable objects where pre-defined models fail.
proposals.method: We will extend category-agnostic reconstruction methods from CARTO and tracking from HANDLOOM. The core is a recurrent neural architecture that fuses RGB-D observations over time into a persistent 3D graph representation. This graph will be updated using a particle-filter-like mechanism, where nodes represent key points and edges encode estimated physical constraints (e.g., inextensibility for cables). Manipulation actions (like fling) will be planned using model-predictive control, with the model's uncertainty driving active perception for state estimation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a unified framework for dynamic state estimation and representation of novel deformable objects, and 2) empirical validation on complex manipulation tasks (e.g., untangling, draping) that are infeasible with static models. It bridges the gap between one-off reconstruction and continual interaction.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that reconstructs an object then plans, our model interleaves perception and representation update *during* manipulation, treating the world model as a latent state to be inferred over time, not a fixed asset. This closes the loop for deformable object manipulation.

proposals.title: Open-Vocabulary Semantic Grounding via Continuous Interaction and Deformable Object Priors
proposals.motivation: While robots can learn object properties through interaction, scaling to large, open-ended vocabularies remains a key challenge. Current methods often rely on curated datasets or predefined object sets, limiting their ability to acquire new word meanings, especially for deformable or articulated items, from ongoing experience. This work addresses the fundamental gap between continuous physical interaction and the acquisition of a vast, expanding semantic inventory.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging a foundation model's semantic prior and coupling it with a state estimation and interaction framework for non-rigid objects, a robot can ground a significantly larger vocabulary of nouns and adjectives through autonomous, curiosity-driven interaction with a diverse set of objects.
proposals.method: We will extend the state estimation techniques from CARTO and HANDLOOM to build a multimodal observation model for diverse objects (rigid, articulated, deformable). A curiosity-driven policy, inspired by 'fling' and other exploratory actions, will generate informative interactions. A pre-trained large language model (LLM) will provide semantic embeddings and propose potential word-object associations, which are then verified or refuted through interaction data, using a contrastive learning objective to align visual, tactile, and kinematic state features with the LLM's embedding space.
proposals.contribution: This will yield a framework for open-ended vocabulary acquisition grounded in physical interaction, moving beyond closed-set object categories. It will provide a novel benchmark and dataset for evaluating semantic grounding at scale and demonstrate how foundation models can be integrated with robotic state estimation for continual learning.
proposals.novelty: The integration of category-agnostic articulated object state estimation (CARTO) with semantic priors from LLMs for open-vocabulary grounding is novel. Unlike prior interactive learning work, this focuses on scaling to thousands of concepts by using the LLM as a scalable semantic memory and hypothesis generator, tested through targeted physical exploration.

proposals.title: Program Induction for Compositional Grounding: Learning Perception-Action Routines from Single Demonstrations and Language
proposals.motivation: Enabling robots to understand and execute open-ended natural language instructions for novel object manipulation remains a core challenge. Current end-to-end learning approaches often lack interpretability and compositional generalization, struggling when objects or tasks differ from training data. A programmatic representation of language, grounded in perception and action, could bridge this gap by enabling systematic recombination of learned skills.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treating natural language commands as programs that induce compositional, executable perception-action routines will enable a robot to generalize manipulation skills to novel objects and scenes from a small number of demonstrations.
proposals.method: We will develop a neuro-symbolic architecture where a language encoder (e.g., a pre-trained LLM like GPT-4) parses an instruction into a program sketch in a domain-specific language (DSL). This sketch will invoke parameterized perceptual primitives (inspired by CARTO for shape, HANDLOOM for cables) and action primitives (like 'fling' or 'grasp'). A program induction module, trained via imitation learning on state-action trajectories (leveraging methods from 'time' and 'state' modeling), will infer the missing perceptual and action parameters from a single demonstration. The complete program can then be re-executed on novel object instances.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for compositional task grounding via program induction, 2) a publicly available benchmark for language-guided manipulation of novel articulated and deformable objects, and 3) empirical validation showing improved sample efficiency and generalization over end-to-end policy learning baselines.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the integration of program induction from demonstration with modern perceptual models for articulated objects, creating an interpretable and compositional middle-ground between monolithic neural policies and manually engineered symbolic systems.

ideas.title: Compositional Scene Physics: From Functional Parts to Room-Level Interaction
ideas.description: This research fuses fine-grained, functional part understanding with holistic scene reconstruction to create a unified model that represents a room not just as a collection of objects and surfaces, but as a compositional hierarchy of interactive, physically-grounded elements. The model jointly infers the 3D pose and shape of object sub-parts (like hinges, drawers, and buttons) and their kinematic properties, while simultaneously reconstructing the room's layout and the poses of all objects within it. This synergy allows the system to reason about potential physical interactions and functional affordances across the entire scene—predicting how parts move, how objects can be used, and how they might be rearranged or collide. The result is a scene representation that supports both detailed manipulation tasks and large-scale spatial planning, bridging the gap between geometric reconstruction and actionable physical understanding.

ideas.title: Learning Web-Scale Disentangled Priors for Semantically-Aware 3D Synthesis and Editing
ideas.description: This research direction proposes training a foundational model on web-scale repositories of 3D models (e.g., Objaverse, ShapeNet) to learn a comprehensive, disentangled prior that encapsulates geometry, texture, and—critically—their intrinsic semantic and functional properties. The synergy emerges by using the scale of data to robustly learn the complex, often non-linear, relationships between an object's form and its purpose (e.g., how 'sit-able' shapes differ from 'grasp-able' ones across object categories). This results in a generative model where users can intuitively synthesize or manipulate 3D content through high-level semantic prompts or affordance labels (e.g., 'make this chair more stable' or 'add a handle for pouring'), achieving a level of contextual awareness and control not possible by learning shape/appearance or semantics in isolation.

ideas.title: Task-Driven Amodal Occupancy Fields for Parsing Extreme Visual Clutter
ideas.description: This approach redefines robustness in cluttered environments by shifting from instance-level detection to a continuous, queryable 4D scene field (3D occupancy + time). The system is trained to model the complete physical extent and latent motion of all matter in a scene, including permanently occluded regions, using multi-modal sensing and synthetic data of piled, tangled, and heaped objects. A task-specific reasoning module then interacts with this field through natural language or functional prompts—like 'clear a path' or 'retrieve a cup'—to generate actionable volumetric segments, effectively parsing the undifferentiated clutter into meaningful, executable structures based on physics and intent.

ideas.title: Open-World Instance Discovery and Reconstruction in Dense Clutter
ideas.description: This research direction develops methods to parse scenes with extreme occlusion and object density where the vast majority of objects belong to categories completely unseen during training. The core challenge is to move beyond mere robustness to known categories and instead achieve open-world instance-level discovery, where the system must distinguish, segment, and potentially reconstruct 3D models of novel objects buried in dense clutter. The synergy creates a capability for autonomous learning of object affordances and structures from raw, complex visual data, forming a foundation for robots or AI to operate in truly novel, unstructured environments like warehouses with unknown items or cluttered natural settings.

ideas.title: Formally Extend G3 with Minimal Representational Upgrades for Complex Linguistic Semantics
ideas.description: This research begins with a formal critique of the G3 framework's capacity to model the semantics of conjunctions, comparatives, and pronouns, identifying specific gaps in handling scope, dependency, and reference. It then proposes a minimal, principled extension to G3's graph structures and update functions, incorporating mechanisms for dynamic binding and relational composition. The enhanced framework is rigorously evaluated on synthetic datasets probing these specific linguistic phenomena to ensure robust generalization, providing a solid foundation before application to naturalistic data.

ideas.title: Socially-Curated Active Grounding for Open-Ended Vocabulary Acquisition
ideas.description: This research develops an agent that learns a vast vocabulary by strategically managing its exposure and actively resolving ambiguity through structured social interaction. Instead of passive continuous learning, the system employs an active learning curriculum, identifying knowledge gaps and uncertainties to formulate targeted queries or physical experiments. This allows it to efficiently solicit disambiguating feedback from the environment or a social partner, grounding rare and abstract terms with high confidence. The result is a sample-efficient, robust grounding mechanism that scales to open-ended vocabularies while minimizing semantic drift and spurious correlations.

ideas.title: Open-World Visual Concept Learning and Novel Category Discovery
ideas.description: This system is trained not to classify a fixed set of categories, but to build a foundational model of visual concepts, parts, and their compositional rules from large-scale data. It uses self-supervised and weakly supervised objectives to learn representations that capture generic objectness, part geometry, and material properties. At inference, it can segment and represent never-before-seen objects using this vocabulary of primitives, and through iterative clustering and relational reasoning, it can group novel instances into self-defined tentative categories, effectively discovering and modeling unknown object types without prior supervision for them.

ideas.title: Semantic Diffusion Priors for Task-Driven 3D Synthesis and Reconstruction
ideas.description: This research direction develops a diffusion-based framework that learns a joint prior over 3D shape, appearance, and semantic or functional properties. The model is trained to denoise not only geometry and texture but also embedded semantic maps or affordance fields, ensuring generated objects adhere to real-world usability constraints. This allows for the task-conditioned synthesis of 3D assets, such as generating a 'chair' that is structurally sound for sitting, or the reconstruction of a partial scan into a complete, functionally coherent object. The core synergy is using the iterative refinement of diffusion models to progressively align low-level geometry with high-level functional requirements, creating a unified prior for intelligent 3D content creation.

ideas.title: Transformer-Based Instance-Aware Grasp Synthesis (TIAGS)
ideas.description: This research fuses a transformer-based instance segmentation model with robotic grasp simulation to create a closed-loop, instance-aware grasp synthesis pipeline. The transformer architecture parses a cluttered scene, predicting precise instance masks and associated semantic or geometric priors, which are used to instantiate approximate physics-ready models directly in a simulation environment. A physics-based grasp planner then evaluates candidate grasps on these reconstructed instances, with the segmentation network's attention mechanisms potentially guiding the search for stable contact points. The key synergy is the bidirectional flow: segmentation informs simulation, while simulation outcomes (e.g., grasp stability metrics) can be fed back to fine-tune the segmentation model for features critical for physical interaction, moving beyond passive perception to active, physics-grounded scene understanding for manipulation.

ideas.title: Incorporate Semantic Priors via a Probabilistic Graphical Model
ideas.description: This idea proposes enhancing 3D shape and appearance priors with semantic or affordance information by drawing an analogy from computational biology's protein structure prediction. In that field, successful methods like Rosetta combine diverse statistical potentials—from low-level atomic distances to high-level structural motifs—into a single probabilistic or energy-based scoring function to find the most plausible 3D fold. Adapted to computer vision and graphics, we would develop a unified energy-based model for object or scene reconstruction. This model would integrate traditional geometric priors (shape, texture) with new semantic/functional prior terms (e.g., defining energy penalties for configurations where a 'chair' lacks a stable seat plane or a 'cup' has no concave volume). The solution is found by optimizing the 3D representation to minimize the total energy, thereby jointly satisfying low-level sensory data and high-level semantic constraints.

ideas.title: Validated Synthesis: A Critic-Guided Framework for Data-Efficient, Physically-Grounded Training
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid training paradigm that overcomes data scarcity not by eliminating synthetic data, but by making it radically more efficient and grounded. A small, curated set of real-world data is used to train a critic model that learns the underlying physical, causal, and statistical constraints of the domain. This critic then guides an adversarial synthesis process, iteratively generating and validating synthetic data points that are maximally informative for the task while remaining within the plausible data manifold. The result is a closed-loop system that produces high-fidelity, task-relevant training data, dramatically reducing the volume of synthetic data required while improving model robustness and generalization in data-poor domains.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Functional Decomposition via Anatomical Segmentation
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from comparative anatomy to computer vision. The analogous problem—understanding complex wholes by identifying recurring functional modules—is solved in biology via hierarchical decomposition into homologous structures analyzed for shape and spatial arrangement. The adapted idea proposes a framework for sub-object understanding that scales down by decomposing objects not into arbitrary segments, but into a learned library of cross-category functional part primitives (e.g., handles, blades, connectors). For a given object, the system would perform a hierarchical segmentation to assign regions to these primitives, simultaneously reconstructing the detailed 3D shape of each part and estimating its 6-DoF pose within a canonical object coordinate frame. This enables a structured, functional representation that generalizes across object instances and categories, supporting reasoning about interaction and affordance.

ideas.title: Generative Deformable Object Synthesis from Latent State Sequences
ideas.description: This research direction develops a generative model that synthesizes realistic, multi-view RGB-D observations of scenes containing deformable objects. The core innovation is an inverse model that takes as input not a single static latent code, but a sequence of latent codes representing the object's state over time, including its shape, pose, and deformation parameters. This allows the model to generate coherent video sequences of objects bending, stretching, or being manipulated, where the visual output is directly controlled by the evolving latent state. The approach moves beyond static scene generation to model and visualize the dynamic, non-rigid interactions central to real-world physical understanding.

ideas.title: Robust Test-Time Adaptation for Unstructured Outdoor Environments via Self-Supervised Scene Dynamics
ideas.description: This research proposes a novel test-time training framework specifically designed for robust perception in unstructured outdoor environments (e.g., forests, construction sites, natural disaster zones). Instead of relying on the static, curated data typical of indoor scenes, the method leverages the inherent temporal dynamics and noisy, multi-modal sensory streams (e.g., video, LiDAR, weather data) of the outdoor world to generate a continuous self-supervision signal. The model is adapted online by learning to predict or reconstruct scene dynamics, making it resilient to domain shifts caused by changing weather, lighting, seasons, and terrain. This enables long-term autonomous operation in the wild where pre-training data is inevitably incomplete and static models fail.

ideas.title: Adapting Hierarchical Assembly from Synthetic Biology to Extend G3 for Complex Syntax
ideas.description: This research adapts the hierarchical assembly approach from synthetic biology to extend the G3 framework. In synthetic biology, complex systems are built by combining standardized, simpler parts (BioBricks) into functional modules with defined interfaces. Analogously, we propose to treat basic G3 semantic primitives as standardized linguistic parts. The solution involves defining a formal protocol for hierarchically assembling these primitives into encapsulated modules for conjunctions, comparatives, and pronouns, with new compositional interface rules governing referential and semantic interactions. This modular, bottom-up approach aims to systematically scale G3's representational capacity to handle more complex linguistic structures while maintaining computational tractability and interpretability.

ideas.title: Vocabulary Acquisition via Predictive, Hierarchical Memory Consolidation
ideas.description: This research develops methods for learning a vast number of word meanings from continuous interaction by drawing an analogy to systems neuroscience. The analogous problem is how the brain acquires and stabilizes a lifetime of episodic memories without catastrophic interference, solved via a multi-stage memory consolidation process involving fast hippocampal encoding and slow neocortical integration guided by predictive replay. The adapted idea implements this as a dual-component AI system: a fast-attention 'buffer' network for online word-referent acquisition during interaction, and a separate, large-scale 'semantic' network for long-term knowledge. An offline consolidation module replays experiences from the buffer, prioritizing items with high predictive error or frequency, to gradually and stably integrate new vocabulary into a hierarchical semantic structure. This enables scalable, continuous learning of an open-ended vocabulary from grounded experience while preserving previously acquired knowledge.

ideas.title: Compositional, Uncertainty-Aware Room Reconstruction with Explicit Occlusion Reasoning
ideas.description: This system reconstructs rooms by treating background structures (walls, floors) and individual objects as separate probabilistic entities, each with an associated confidence map. A relational scene graph explicitly models probable spatial relationships and occlusions between these entities, informed by physical priors. The framework continuously integrates new, partial observations by updating entity models and their relational uncertainties, enabling robust completion even with significant initial occlusions or sparse sensor data. This results in a scene representation that is both detailed and explicitly aware of its own reconstruction confidence and gaps.

ideas.title: Progressive Domain Adaptation with Failure-Aware Fallback for Unstructured Outdoor Navigation
ideas.description: This research develops a system for robust operation in unstructured outdoor environments by directly addressing the domain gap from indoor scenes. It uses a progressive training curriculum that transitions from indoor-like semi-structured areas to fully wild settings, teaching the model to handle increasing variability. A key component is a concurrently trained failure-prediction network that estimates epistemic uncertainty and triggers a safe, heuristic-based fallback mode when confidence is low. This approach ensures the system does not blindly trust its indoor-derived models, enabling reliable and safe exploration and task execution in novel outdoor domains.

ideas.title: Zero-Shot Object Recognition via Taxonomic Projection from Foundational Models
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that models must be trained on examples of all object categories they will encounter. Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology, where new species are classified by fitting them into an existing phylogenetic tree based on conserved traits, the proposed solution adapts this to computer vision. The core idea is to train a model not on fixed categories, but to build or leverage a foundational semantic space (e.g., from a vision-language model) that encodes relationships between visual features and abstract attributes. During inference, a completely unknown object is projected into this space. Its category is not retrieved from a closed set but is inferred by its position relative to known categories—identifying it as a novel node within a known taxonomic or conceptual structure, enabling zero-shot recognition of never-before-seen object types.

ideas.title: Adapting Kalman Filtering from Control Theory for Dynamic Scene Reconstruction
ideas.description: This idea generalizes the original method to handle moving and articulated objects by drawing an analogy from Control Theory. The analogous solution is the Kalman Filter, a recursive algorithm for state estimation in dynamic systems. The adapted idea integrates a Kalman Filtering framework into the reconstruction pipeline, where the 3D pose, motion, and articulation parameters form the system state. A prediction step uses a dynamics model to propagate the state between frames, and an update step corrects it with visual evidence from the new frame. This provides a principled, robust approach to maintaining temporal coherence and handling uncertainty in dynamic scene reconstruction from video sequences.

ideas.title: Adapting Robust Control Theory from Aerospace to Unstructured Robotic Environments
ideas.description: The original idea challenges the assumption of domain-limited indoor scenes by proposing application to outdoor or unstructured environments. Drawing an analogy from Aerospace Engineering and Control Theory, where robust and adaptive control systems maintain performance despite severe, unpredictable external disturbances like turbulence. The analogous solution involves formally defining a bounded set of possible disturbances and designing controllers guaranteed to be stable across that entire set. Adapted to robotics and computer vision research, this leads to an idea where perception and navigation algorithms for outdoor robots are not just trained on more data, but are architected with formal robustness guarantees. The system would define an operational envelope encompassing variables like precipitation, lighting gradients, and moving foliage, and employ adaptive frameworks (inspired by MRAC or H-infinity methods) to adjust perception models or planning strategies in real-time, ensuring reliable core functionality is maintained even when perfect accuracy is impossible.

ideas.title: Factorized Diffusion for Disentangled Shape and Appearance Priors
ideas.description: This research introduces a factorized diffusion model framework designed to learn disentangled and composable priors for 3D shape and appearance. Instead of a single denoising process, the system employs separate diffusion models for shape (geometry) and key appearance attributes (e.g., albedo, roughness), linked through a lightweight, cross-attention based coupling module. This structure enforces a factorized latent space, enabling independent manipulation and sampling of shape and material properties. The resulting prior is more expressive for inverse graphics tasks, allowing for targeted editing, efficient few-shot learning, and robust reconstruction by leveraging the appropriate compositional factors.

ideas.title: Adaptive Hierarchical Attention for Extreme Clutter via Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the ecological concept of Niche Modeling to computer vision for extreme clutter. In ecology, predicting a species in a dense forest involves modeling key environmental gradients rather than counting every tree and leaf. Similarly, we propose to train a model to identify the fundamental 'visual niche' of a target object. The system would learn a hierarchy of attention mechanisms over layered feature maps representing different 'gradients' of the visual scene (e.g., edge density fields, material texture maps, occlusion boundary likelihoods). A probabilistic model, inspired by ecological maximum entropy approaches, would then integrate these gradients to predict the likelihood of an object's presence at each location, even when it is heavily occluded. This shifts the paradigm from direct instance recognition in clutter to searching for the unique signature of conditions where the object is likely to exist, thereby improving robustness in scenes with extreme occlusion and object density.

ideas.title: Dual-Scale Protein-Ligand Docking for Robotic Grasp Simulation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the hierarchical, dual-scale solution from computational molecular docking to the problem of physics-based robotic grasp planning. In the analogous field, fast coarse-grained sampling is followed by high-fidelity physics-based refinement to predict protein-ligand binding. Adapted here, a robotic grasp planner would first use a fast, possibly learning-based or geometric, coarse simulator to explore the vast space of potential grasps on a reconstructed object model, generating many candidates. The most promising of these candidates are then passed to a slower, high-fidelity physics engine that utilizes the detailed geometry and estimated physical properties from the reconstruction for precise simulation. This dual-scale approach balances computational efficiency with the accuracy needed for reliable, physics-based grasp prediction in complex scenarios.

ideas.title: Physically Constrained Scene Synthesis from Object Latent Codes
ideas.description: This adversarially improved system generates realistic and physically plausible RGB-D images from sets of object latent codes. It introduces a structured scene layout predictor that enforces geometric and support constraints between objects, preventing implausible arrangements. A subsequent differentiable renderer synthesizes the final image by modeling global illumination and consistent shadow casting based on the unified scene layout. This ensures the output observations are not just visually realistic but also adhere to the physical laws of object interaction and lighting.

ideas.title: Continuum Mechanics-Inspired Deformable Object Representation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the rigid object assumption by adapting the core solution from continuum mechanics. In that field, the deformation of materials (from steel to rubber) is solved by modeling a continuous displacement field governed by constitutive laws, then discretizing for computation. Adapted to computer vision and robotics, this means representing an object not with a single rigid transform but with a parameterized deformation field defined over a template 3D shape. The research would develop methods to learn or define plausible deformation models (analogous to constitutive laws) and algorithms to estimate the field's parameters from sensor data (images, point clouds), enabling systems to perceive, track, and interact with objects that bend, stretch, or deform in complex ways.

ideas.title: Apply transformer-based instance segmentation using a protein folding-inspired attention mechanism
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from computational biology's protein structure prediction problem, specifically AlphaFold2's transformer-based architecture. The analogous solution uses an Evoformer module to refine pairwise residue representations through attention, enabling long-range interactions to determine local 3D structure. Adapted to computer vision, we propose a transformer architecture for instance segmentation that processes a set of initial instance mask queries. By using self-attention among queries and cross-attention between queries and image features, the model can leverage global contextual information from the entire image to directly and simultaneously predict precise, final instance masks, moving beyond the typical detect-then-segment pipeline.

ideas.title: Hybrid CNN-Transformer with Deformable Attention for Efficient Instance Segmentation
ideas.description: This redesigned system combines the spatial efficiency of a convolutional neural network (CNN) backbone with the relational modeling of a transformer in a computationally feasible pipeline. The CNN extracts multi-scale feature maps, while a set of object queries interacts with these features through a transformer decoder equipped with deformable attention, which focuses on a small set of key sampling points per query. This hybrid design maintains the global context and parallel decoding advantages of transformers but at a fraction of the computational cost of a pure transformer mask decoder. The result is a robust instance segmentation model suitable for real-time or high-resolution applications where the original idea would be intractable.

ideas.title: Leveraging Domain Adaptation and Active Learning from Ecological Surveying
ideas.description: This idea addresses the core challenge of reducing reliance on simulated training data by drawing an analogy from ecological surveying. In ecology, scientists efficiently study new, data-scarce environments by combining targeted field sampling (active learning) with knowledge transfer from analogous, well-studied ecosystems (domain adaptation). Adapted to machine learning, the research would develop techniques that integrate active learning to strategically query and label the most valuable real-world data, thereby reducing the volume of data needed. Concurrently, it would advance domain adaptation methods to maximally leverage pre-existing models trained on related, abundant real-world source domains, enabling effective training on the target task with minimal or no synthetic data, thus improving model robustness and real-world applicability.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Gated Test-Time Adaptation with a Protected Prior
ideas.description: This system performs safe, online adaptation for a specific scene by decoupling adaptation from the core model's stable knowledge. A small adapter network is trained on-the-fly using a self-supervised task (e.g., reconstruction or consistency across photometric augmentations) applied to the test sequence. The adapter's influence on the final output is modulated by the core model's predictive uncertainty, ensuring adaptation primarily occurs where the prior is weak. The core model's parameters are regularized using a soft weight constraint or a distillation loss from its initial state, preserving general world knowledge and preventing catastrophic forgetting.

ideas.title: Differentiable Physics-Constrained Neural Deformation Fields
ideas.description: This approach models non-rigid objects using a neural radiance field (NeRF) architecture where the deformation field is parameterized by a low-dimensional latent code. A key component is a lightweight, differentiable physics simulator (e.g., a mass-spring or co-rotational FEM model) that runs in parallel, acting as a soft constraint on the learned deformation to ensure physical plausibility like volume preservation and smooth strain. The system is trained end-to-end from multi-view video, learning both the object's appearance and a latent deformation space where each axis corresponds to an interpretable, physically-valid mode of deformation, thereby making tracking and future state prediction tractable.

ideas.title: Inverse Rendering via Geophysical Seismic Inversion
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the geophysical inverse problem of seismic inversion to the domain of computer vision. In seismology, subsurface physical properties are estimated from seismic recordings via full waveform inversion—a process that iteratively adjusts a subsurface model so that simulated seismic data matches observations. Analogously, we propose to solve the vision inverse problem of generating realistic RGB-D images from sets of object latent codes. This is achieved by constructing a differentiable forward renderer (the analogue of a wave propagation simulator) that synthesizes RGB-D images from latent codes. The core method involves treating a target RGB-D image as the 'observed data' and optimizing the latent codes via gradient-based techniques (the analogue of seismic inversion optimization) to minimize the discrepancy between the rendered and target images, thereby learning to infer compact scene representations from visual observations.

ideas.title: Monocular Depth Inference via Atmospheric Scattering Models
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that depth estimation requires RGB-D input by proposing to use only a single RGB image. The analogy is drawn from astronomy and remote sensing, where the depth of atmospheric phenomena is inferred from a single 2D image using physical models of light scattering and attenuation through the atmosphere. The adapted idea is to treat a general scene as if viewed through a unifying visual medium and develop a model that learns an 'in-scene scattering' prior. This model would interpret the subtle variations in color, contrast, and texture within a single RGB image—akin to how atmospheric scattering affects light over distance—to directly regress a depth map, providing a monocular depth estimation solution without depth sensor data.

ideas.title: Apply hierarchical diffusion for multi-scale shape and appearance priors
ideas.description: This idea adapts the multi-scale inversion methodology from geophysics and seismic imaging to the problem of learning expressive shape and appearance priors with diffusion models. In seismic tomography, the subsurface is reconstructed hierarchically: low-frequency data first resolves large-scale basin structures, then higher-frequency data incrementally adds finer details like faults and stratigraphy, ensuring the global structure is correct before refining details. Adapted to diffusion models, this means designing a hierarchical diffusion process that learns priors at multiple scales. The model would first learn a prior over coarse, global shape and low-frequency appearance components. Subsequent stages would then learn to refine this prior, adding medium-scale geometry and mid-frequency texture, and finally fine-scale details and high-frequency appearance. This structured, multi-scale approach to prior learning can yield more robust and expressive priors by explicitly separating and learning dependencies across different scales of variation, preventing the common pitfall where local detail generation is misaligned with global structure.

ideas.title: Scene-Specific Online Adaptation via Self-Supervised Consistency Loss
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from adaptive optics in astronomy, which continuously corrects a telescope's mirrors based on real-time measurements of atmospheric distortion, this idea adapts a model online for a specific scene using test-time training. The analogous solution uses a guide star and a control loop to adjust optics; here, the model's own predictions on the test sequence serve as the guide. The adapted research idea is to implement scene-specific online adaptation via a self-supervised consistency loss (e.g., augmentation invariance, temporal smoothness) computed solely on the test data, followed by a lightweight optimization loop that updates model parameters to minimize this loss, thereby customizing the model to the unique properties of the test scene in real-time.

ideas.title: Multi-Resolution Hierarchical Reconstruction for Room-Scale Scenes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the hierarchical, multi-resolution simulation techniques from cosmology (like Adaptive Mesh Refinement) to the problem of scaling up to room-level scene reconstruction. The analogous astronomical solution involves modeling vast cosmic volumes at low resolution for context, while refining resolution selectively in critical regions to capture detailed physics. Adapted here, the research would develop a joint reconstruction framework that first establishes a low-fidelity model of the entire room's background structure (walls, floor, ceiling). This global context then guides the adaptive, high-resolution reconstruction of individual objects and complex surfaces within the room, all maintained within a unified representation. This method efficiently scales computational effort, focusing detail where it matters most while ensuring a consistent, holistic scene model.

ideas.title: Fast Neural Feature Grid Refinement with Amortized Ray Sampling
ideas.description: This redesigned approach replaces the slow, vanilla NeRF optimization with a tailored, efficient neural representation for refinement. It uses a multi-resolution hash-encoded feature grid (like InstantNGP) for rapid querying, coupled with a task-specific, lightweight rendering network. Crucially, the system implements an amortized ray sampling strategy that caches and updates importance sampling distributions and visibility buffers across optimization steps, drastically reducing the number of samples needed per iteration. This enables neural refinement at speeds comparable to or faster than traditional analysis-by-synthesis, while retaining the benefits of a continuous, high-fidelity scene model.

ideas.title: Adapt Seismic Tomography's Waveform Inversion to NeRF-based Scene Refinement
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of full waveform inversion from seismic tomography to the problem of 3D scene refinement. In geophysics, FWI improves subsurface models by matching the full simulated seismic waveform to observed data, not just discrete travel times. Analogously, we propose a NeRF-based refinement that goes beyond standard photometric analysis-by-synthesis. The method defines a comprehensive 'neural radiance field loss' that compares the model's behavior—its entire volumetric density and view-dependent emission profile—against multi-view observations. By employing an adjoint optimization through the NeRF's differentiable volume rendering, gradients are computed to update the neural representation, ensuring the refined scene is globally consistent across all viewpoints, similar to how FWI ensures wavefield consistency. This leads to a more robust and higher-fidelity refinement than pixel-level matching.
